Like “find all the API endpoints AND all the React components at the same time” — both Subagents run concurrently, much faster than doing them one at a time.

What kinds of tasks don’t fit the Agent tool

Agent isn’t a silver bullet though. Some cases don’t fit:

  • Tasks that need back-and-forth discussion: Subagents can’t see the main conversation history, so every dispatch you have to re-explain the background. For things that need lots of iteration, Subagent actually slows you down
  • Single-file edits with a clear goal: Just Read and Edit yourself — handing off to a Subagent is the long way around
  • Tasks that genuinely depend on the main conversation’s context: If you can’t be bothered re-explaining, just let the main conversation handle it

Note
If you don’t want Claude to auto-dispatch a particular Subagent, add a rule like Agent(Explore) to permissions.deny. The syntax only blocks that specific Subagent — others still work.

Other tools worth knowing

Above were the day-to-day heavy hitters. Here are a few that show up less often but are still worth knowing.

WebFetch and WebSearch

The names look similar but they do different things. WebFetch takes a URL and pulls the page down as Markdown — good for “I have an official doc URL, please follow it” cases. WebSearch actually searches keywords — good for “how do I use the latest version of some API” cases where you need to find information.

If you tell Claude “look up Vue 3.5’s latest features,” it’ll usually WebSearch first to find the official docs URL, then WebFetch to grab the full content. That’s a common combo move.

AskUserQuestion

When Claude hits a choice it’s not sure about, it sometimes pops a question at you — that’s the AskUserQuestion tool. It’s multiple-choice. For instance, if you ask it to scaffold a project but didn’t specify React or Vue, it might pause and offer you a few options instead of guessing.

If you’d rather it not stop to ask, write your defaults into CLAUDE.md and it’ll decide for itself instead of popping up.

LSP

LSP is a newer tool (via Language Server Protocol), good for code intelligence — jumping to function definitions, finding where a symbol is referenced, listing implementations of an interface, things like that. The most noticeable effect is that after every Edit, LSP automatically reports type errors and warnings for that file, so Claude doesn’t have to run a build separately to know whether it broke something.

Heads up though — this tool only kicks in if you’ve installed the Code Intelligence Plugin for the relevant language. Without it, Claude can’t see anything from LSP.

Monitor

Monitor lets Claude run a command in the background and streams every output line back as it happens, good for:

  • Watching a log file and pinging when an error shows up
  • Monitoring CI status changes
  • Watching a folder for new files

The difference vs Bash’s run_in_background — Monitor “keeps feeding data to Claude,” while Bash background “notifies once when it’s done.”

TaskCreate / TaskUpdate / TaskList

These are how Claude manages task lists internally. When a task is complex, it builds a todo list and ticks off items one by one. When you see Claude pulling up a checklist in the Claude Code UI, that’s these tools at work.

A small detail here — interactive sessions (the everyday claude command) use TaskCreate / TaskUpdate / TaskList, while Headless mode and the Agent SDK use an older tool called TodoWrite. Same concept, different implementation.

How does Claude decide which tool to use?

Now that we’ve covered the tools, how does Claude actually decide?

Short version — there are two sources.

The first is the System Prompt. Claude Code loads a long system prompt at startup that explicitly says which tools to prefer and what each tool’s constraints are — rules like “if you can use a built-in tool, don’t use Bash” or “Read before Edit.” So part of Claude’s tool-picking logic is taught up front.

The second is Claude’s own judgment. The model uses your request, the current conversation context, and the file info in Context to guess which tool fits best. This part is where Prompt clarity matters — the vaguer your Prompt, the more likely Claude takes a detour.

A concrete example to make it click:

  • You say “open src/auth/login.ts“ → Claude goes straight to Read because the path is explicit
  • You say “find the login logic” → Claude probably Globs **/login* or **/auth/** first, falls back to Grep if nothing matches
  • You say “scan the whole project for usage of deprecated APIs” → A smarter response is dispatching the Explore Subagent

From this angle, the more specific your Prompt, the more accurately Claude uses tools. That’s exactly why I wanted you to learn how to read tool usage patterns earlier.

Reverse-engineering your Prompt from how Claude uses tools

This is the heart of this post. Everything above was setup for this.

Claude keeps Read-ing different files without finding the target

Usually means your description was too abstract and it’s guessing at file locations. For example, you say “fix the home page,” but it has no idea which file the home page is, so it keeps Read-ing things that look home-page-shaped.

Fix — give the path or a precise description in your Prompt, like “fix the search logic in src/pages/Home.vue.”

Claude keeps Glob-ing and finds nothing

Means your keywords don’t line up with the actual filenames. Like you say “find login-related files” but the project actually uses Auth* naming, so Globbing for *login* finds nothing.

Fix — tell it the naming convention, or switch to Grep on the import strings — that’s usually more reliable.

Claude keeps failing Edits and falls back to Write

If you see Claude trying Edit a few times, getting “old_string not found” errors, and finally switching to Write, two common causes:

  1. You edited the file in your IDE in the meantime — Claude’s remembered content doesn’t match disk
  2. Inconsistent indentation or trailing whitespace (especially tabs vs spaces)

Fix — tell it to Read the file again before editing, or be explicit: “please Read the latest content first before editing.”

Claude doesn’t dispatch a Subagent and grinds through files itself

If a task should’ve gone to Agent (like a project-wide scan) but Claude is Read-ing files one by one, it’s because it didn’t recognize this as a big task.

Fix — write it directly into your Prompt: “please use the Explore subagent to scan the whole project for me.” Be explicit about wanting a Subagent dispatched.

Claude keeps detouring through Bash

If it should be Read but it’s running cat, or it should be Grep but it’s running grep, it’s usually one of these:

  • permissions.deny is blocking the built-in tools and forcing Claude to fall back to Bash (remember, the deny rule only blocks built-ins, not Bash)
  • There are unnecessary nudges in CLAUDE.md
  • The thing it’s doing genuinely needs a pipe combo, like “find the five largest log files”

The first two can be fixed via /permissions and reviewing CLAUDE.md. The third is fine — let it be.

Wrap-up

Quick recap of the main points:

  • Claude Code’s built-in Tools mostly fall into reading (Read, Glob, Grep), editing (Edit, Write), executing (Bash), and delegating (Agent), plus newer ones like LSP, Monitor, and TaskCreate
  • Read brings line numbers, Edit needs Read first, and use Write sparingly to avoid overwriting your manual edits
  • Glob finds filename patterns, Grep finds content strings — getting these straight stops Claude from picking wrong
  • Agent is the most powerful and most overlooked tool. Claude Code has three built-in Subagents — Explore, Plan, and general-purpose. For tasks that need to read lots of files, Explore is the most cost-effective
  • permissions.deny rules for Read/Edit only block the built-ins, not Bash subprocesses. To block at the OS level, go with sandboxing
  • Once you can read which tool Claude picked, you can reverse-engineer where your Prompt was unclear

Hope this gave you a clearer picture of Claude Code’s built-in Tools — next time it picks the wrong tool, you’ll know how to tweak the Prompt.
entry.summary: This post is a quick chat about Claude Code's built-in Tools. If you've used Claude Code, you've probably noticed something — sometimes when you ask it to read a file, it just opens it with Read. Other times when you ask "what components does this project have," it goes off and runs Glob and Grep. And occasionally, it takes a detour through Bash's cat or grep, which is confusing because the built-in tools are right there. Claude actually has its own logic for picking tools — this post walks through what built-in Tools Claude Code has, how it decides which one to use, and how to reverse-engineer its tool usage to fix your prompts.

entry.title: Want to Start Vibe Coding? The Fundamentals Beginners Should Get Down First
entry.content:
Intro

Lately a lot of friends have been asking me, “I want to use AI to build things too — where do I start?” And what they’re really asking about is what people are calling Vibe Coding. This post is about the fundamentals beginners should get down if you want to step into the Vibe Coding world — Git, command line, UI Frameworks, and so on. So you don’t get halfway through and find you’re stuck not knowing how to communicate with the AI.

Wait, didn’t we say Vibe Coding doesn’t need any technical knowledge?

Before we dive into the fundamentals, let me address this myth.

If you haven’t read my earlier post A quick take on AI Vibe Coding’s mainstream methodologies — BDD and SDD, check it out — it covers what Vibe Coding is and the two mainstream development frameworks more thoroughly.

In short, Vibe Coding is the dev style of “describe a feature directly in natural language and let AI generate the code for you”. The key point is you don’t have to write code yourself — just describe what you want to the AI.

Sounds great, right?

But let me throw a bit of cold water on that — Vibe Coding doesn’t mean “you don’t need to know any technology at all”.

A real example I’ve seen: a friend used AI to build a small website. It got built and they were happy, but one day, while the AI was editing things, it accidentally broke the entire homepage. The friend had to delete everything and start over because they had no idea how to roll back to the previous working version.

That’s why you still need to know some fundamentals — when the AI screws up, at least you know how to save yourself.

So… what should beginners learn? Below I’ll go through them one by one, roughly in order of “likelihood of getting tripped up” — earlier ones are more important.

Note
This post tries to explain everything in the most plain way possible — assuming you’ve never written code before. If you have dev experience, some sections might feel basic. That’s intentional, please don’t mind.

Git and GitHub: your undo button

If you only take away one thing from this whole post, I’d recommend you learn Git first.

What is Git? In short, Git is a “version control tool” that records every change you make to your files. So when the AI breaks your code, you can always roll back to the last working version.

Using a more everyday analogy: Git is like save points in a video game. After clearing each level, you save. So if you die in the next level, you can go back to the last save point and try again — instead of restarting the whole game.

What about GitHub? GitHub is a cloud platform for storing Git records. You can put your project up there — not just for backup but also to sync between different computers and collaborate with others.

For beginners, at minimum learn these basic actions:

ActionDescription
git initInitialize Git tracking in a project folder
git addStage files to be saved
git commitActually save — every save needs a message describing what changed
git statusSee which files haven’t been saved yet
git diffSee what you changed
git logSee the history of saves

From this table you can see the core concept of Git is “saving”. Master these few actions and you can handle 80% of your needs.

Note
If you really don’t want to touch the command line, VSCode has a built-in Git visual interface, and GitHub has GitHub Desktop as a graphical tool. But I’d still recommend learning the commands first, since most AI tool tutorials are presented through commands — being able to read commands helps you a lot.

As for advanced stuff like git branch, git merge, you can learn those once you’re more comfortable. Don’t try to cram everything in at once.

Terminal (command line): the gateway to interacting with tools

A lot of beginners get scared the first time they see that all-black window with a blinking cursor — but in the Vibe Coding world, you can’t escape the Terminal.

What is a Terminal? Let me briefly explain two terms:

  • GUI (Graphical User Interface): apps you use daily like LINE, Word, Chrome — there are buttons and icons, and you click with your mouse to operate.
  • CLI (Command Line Interface): no buttons, no icons — the entire screen is just lines of text. You type commands to tell the computer what to do.

Terminal is the latter — usually translated as “命令列” or “終端機” in Taiwan. In short, it’s a window that lets you type to communicate with the computer.

Why do engineers still use Terminal when GUIs are so convenient? Because Terminal can directly execute very fine-grained commands faster than clicking through with a mouse, and many dev tools simply don’t have a graphical interface — you can only operate them through Terminal.

So why can’t beginners escape it? Because mainstream AI Coding tools like Claude Code are themselves tools that run in the Terminal — you open Terminal and type claude to launch it. Other tools like Cursor are editors but have a built-in Terminal too — you’ll still use it.

What commands do beginners need to learn at minimum? Just these:

CommandUse
cdSwitch folders, e.g., cd my-project
lsSee files in the current folder (Windows uses dir)
pwdSee which folder you’re currently in
mkdirCreate a new folder
clearClear the screen

Not many commands, but they’re a really important entry point for beginners. Because when you don’t know which folder you’re in and an AI tool is running, you’ll be confused about where it’s actually executing things.

Note
macOS’s built-in Terminal is usable, but I’d recommend installing iTerm2 with Oh My Zsh — much more visually pleasant. For Windows, you can use Windows Terminal with WSL — way better experience than the default PowerShell.

Editor: VSCode is basically the default now

Even though Vibe Coding’s pitch is “no need to write code yourself”, you still need a place to open files, see what the AI wrote, and manually tweak when needed — that’s the editor.

The most mainstream free editor right now is VSCode (Visual Studio Code), developed by Microsoft, cross-platform, with a huge ecosystem and the best AI tool integration of any editor.

After installing VSCode, I’d recommend installing at least these extensions:

  • GitHub Copilot: real-time code completion. Vibe Coding doesn’t usually involve you typing, but having it during review is smoother.
  • GitLens: super intuitive Git history viewer — you can see who changed each line and when.
  • Prettier: auto-formats your code nicely.
  • Language-specific extensions: Volar (Vue), ESLint (JavaScript), etc. — install based on your project needs.

As for Cursor, Kiro, Antigravity — these are AI editors based on VSCode. If you’ve used VSCode and want to switch over, there’s basically no learning curve. But for beginners, I’d still suggest starting with VSCode, since most tutorials use VSCode for demos.

Note
If you want to know how mainstream AI Coding tools differ, check out my earlier post Which AI Coding Tool Should You Pick in 2026? Copilot vs Cursor vs Claude Code vs Kiro vs Antigravity.

A little HTML, CSS, JavaScript

Seeing this section you might push back: “Didn’t you say no need to write code?”

Don’t get me wrong — I’m not saying you need to write it. You just need to “read” it.

Let’s go back to a more basic question: what is a web page made of?

When you open a website and see titles, images, buttons, tables — those are all written in code. Just like books are made of letters, web pages are made of lines of code.

Building web pages mainly uses three programming languages (or markup languages). The names sound scary, but each one’s job is pretty intuitive:

  • HTML: handles “what’s on the page” — like “put a title here, put an image there, put a button here”
  • CSS: handles “what these things look like” — like “title in red, button with rounded corners, image centered”
  • JavaScript: handles “what these things do” — like “when the button is clicked, pop up a window; when scrolled to the bottom of the page, load more articles”

Using a house-building analogy: HTML is like the steel and concrete of a house, defining where the walls, doors, and windows go. CSS is like the interior decoration — what color to paint, what flooring to use, how to arrange the furniture. JavaScript is like the electric devices — flip a switch to turn on lights, sensors detect someone passing to auto-open the door.

You don’t need to know how to write it, but you should at least know “ah this part is HTML, that part is CSS”. Because when you want to tell the AI “change this button to blue”, you’ll know what needs to be changed is CSS (appearance), not HTML (structure).

A more concrete example. Say the AI gives you this code:

If you can’t read it at all, you can only tell the AI “my button isn’t right” — but the AI doesn’t know what “isn’t right” means. If you can read that the above is “a red-background, white-text Submit button”, you can directly say “I want the button color changed to blue”, and the AI will go change the red in that background-color line.

How much should you learn? I’d suggest at minimum going through the intro tutorials for HTML and CSS on MDN once. For JavaScript, just understand the basic concepts of “variables”, “if-else”, “loops”, “functions” — don’t try to actually write from scratch.

Frontend frameworks: you’ll hear React, Vue, Svelte mentioned

This next one is where beginners most often get confused.

When you tell the AI “build me a web page”, the AI doesn’t simply throw HTML/CSS/JavaScript at you. It very likely picks a frontend framework — React, Vue, Svelte, etc. These are extensions of JavaScript, not different languages. But on first contact, beginners get scared by the weird syntax.

So what is a frontend framework?

In short, a frontend framework is “a set of JavaScript tools that help you build web interfaces more efficiently”. It splits a web page into individual “components” that you can reuse, instead of writing HTML/CSS from scratch every time.

What’s a “component”? Here’s an example: when you open YouTube, the like count, like button, share button under each video — every video looks the same, right? That’s a component. Engineers write it once and apply it to all videos. To change something, you only need to change one place.

Using a more everyday analogy: without a frontend framework is like cooking from scratch by growing rice every time. A frontend framework directly hands you white rice and meal kits — you just assemble and have a dish.

The current most mainstream frontend frameworks are roughly:

FrameworkDescription
ReactDeveloped by Meta, currently highest market share, most complete ecosystem, medium learning curve
VueQuite popular in Taiwan and Asia, syntax is relatively intuitive, beginner-friendly
SvelteA newer choice, no Virtual DOM means great performance, very concise syntax
AngularDeveloped by Google, heavier, used more in enterprise projects

Why should beginners know these? Because when AI-generated code shows up with weird-looking syntax like useState, onMounted, $:, you know which framework it’s using — so you know how to ask the AI and where to look up docs.

Otherwise you can only tell the AI “I don’t get this part”, and the AI doesn’t know which part you don’t get either.

Note
If you don’t have a strong preference, I’d suggest beginners start with Vue. Its learning curve is gentler and there are lots of resources in Traditional Chinese. If you’re already heading toward job-seeking, React has higher market demand.

UI Framework: tools that beautify your UI — Tailwind, Bootstrap, shadcn

After frontend frameworks, let’s get familiar with something that’s easily confused with them — UI Frameworks.

What’s a UI Framework? In short, “a set of tools that help you quickly build nice-looking interfaces”. It provides ready-made components (buttons, tables, cards, etc.) or a pre-designed style system, so you don’t have to handcraft CSS from scratch.

What’s the difference from a frontend framework?

Using the cooking analogy again: a frontend framework gives you white rice and a kitchen so you can cook all kinds of dishes. A UI Framework is more like pre-made sauce packs — drizzle it on and you’ve got that flavor without slowly seasoning yourself. The frontend framework handles “structure”, the UI Framework handles “appearance” — they can be used together.

Mainstream UI Frameworks roughly fall into these categories:

NameStyleDescription
Tailwind CSSUtility class-orientedDoesn’t give ready-made components — gives you tons of small utility classes to combine freely
BootstrapComponent-orientedOld, stable, provides ready-made buttons, navs, forms
Material UI (MUI)Component-orientedGoogle’s Material Design style, paired with React
shadcn/uiCopy-paste styleCopies component source code directly into your project so you can modify freely
DaisyUIComponent-orientedComponent library built on Tailwind

Beginners don’t need to know all UI Frameworks at once — just learn whichever one you encounter first.

If you have no idea, I’d recommend starting with Tailwind CSS. A lot of AI tools default to writing in Tailwind, and Tailwind + shadcn/ui is basically the default choice for new projects in 2026.

The benefit of knowing these differences is when you read AI-generated code, you’ll know which classes are Tailwind and which are custom — so when you change colors or spacing, you don’t change the wrong place.

Node.js and package managers

When you start building things with AI, sooner or later you’ll hit the npm install command, then a node_modules folder appears with hundreds of things you don’t recognize.

Don’t panic — that’s the Node.js world.

What’s Node.js?

I mentioned earlier that JavaScript is the language responsible for making web pages interactive, but originally it could only run inside browsers — it couldn’t run directly on your computer. Node.js made it possible for JavaScript to leave the browser and run directly on your computer — like turning a fish that could only swim in water into an amphibian that can go anywhere.

Modern frontend dev tools mostly depend on Node.js to run (including a lot of helper programs for AI tools), so you still need to install it.

What’s a package manager? Imagine cooking — you wouldn’t brew your own soy sauce, right? You’d buy a bottle from the supermarket. A package manager is the supermarket of the code world — it downloads code other people wrote for you to use.

The mainstream package managers right now:

ToolDescription
npmBuilt into Node.js, oldest and most universal
yarnReleased by Facebook, was once much faster than npm — the gap isn’t as obvious now
pnpmUses hard links to share packages — saves disk space and installs fast, very popular recently

For me personally, I mainly use pnpm now because it really saves disk space when handling lots of projects.

A few files you’ll definitely encounter:

  • package.json: your project’s “ID card”, recording the project name and which packages it uses
  • package-lock.json / pnpm-lock.yaml: locks each package’s version, ensuring you and others have the same install
  • node_modules: where packages actually get downloaded and stored. This folder usually gets large, so it’s not committed to Git.

Beginners don’t need to understand too much at first — knowing “npm install installs packages” and “npm run dev starts the dev server” handles most needs.

Reading error messages

This one looks simple but is actually a critical skill in the Vibe Coding process.

Why? Because the typical Vibe Coding flow looks like this:

  1. You describe the feature you want
  2. AI writes the code
  3. You try running it
  4. Errors happen
  5. You feed the error message back to the AI
  6. AI fixes based on the error message

Notice step 5 — if you can’t read error messages, or don’t even know where to find them, the entire flow breaks.

The most common problem beginners hit: “My web page didn’t react when I opened it, but I don’t see any errors!”

That’s because error messages don’t pop up automatically — they hide in something called “DevTools”. Press F12 on any web page (or right-click and choose “Inspect”), and a side panel pops up. That panel is DevTools — your gateway to “the world behind the web page”.

DevTools has many tabs. The two most commonly used by beginners:

  • Console: when code has problems, red error messages show up here. The first thing beginners need to learn to read.
  • Network: records of the web page fetching data from the backend. When the page can’t get data, check here to see if the API is broken.

Beyond the browser, error messages also appear in:

  • Terminal window: the black box where you ran commands. Failed package installs, server start failures, compile errors — all printed here.
  • Editor’s Problems tab: at the bottom of VSCode there’s a Problems tab showing syntax errors.

The thing beginners most easily ignore is the browser Console. Many people see no reaction when opening a web page and assume the AI broke it. Then they hit F12 and the Console is already red and screaming about the problem.

Copying the entire error message back to the AI is the fastest fix. Don’t just tell the AI “my thing is broken” — that makes the AI guess again. Just paste that red English along with the surrounding context to the AI, and it’ll know where the problem is.

Environment variables and basic security

I’m pulling this out specifically because beginners hit this gotcha most often, and it really hurts when they do.

Let me first explain what an “API Key” is, since beginners might not know.

In short, an API Key is like “a membership card a service issues to you”. You use this card to access cloud services like OpenAI, Google Maps, etc. Every time you use the card, the service notes how much you used and charges you (or deducts from your free quota).

So this card is “tied to your money”. If someone else takes it, they can use these services on your dime.

What does this mean? Say you ask AI to build a small tool that connects to the OpenAI API. AI might write:

Looks fine, right? But if you write it this way and push the code to a public GitHub repo, your API Key is publicly exposed. Within minutes, bots will likely scrape it and abuse it — and by the time you notice, the bill is hundreds of dollars.

The right approach is to put these secrets in “environment variables”, commonly stored in a file called .env:

Then in code, you read it like this:

Then add .env to .gitignore so Git doesn’t commit this file:

These basic concepts beginners must establish:

  • Don’t put API Keys, passwords, Tokens directly in code
  • Put secrets in .env and add .env to .gitignore
  • Don’t push .env to GitHub

What if you’ve already pushed it accidentally? The fastest fix is to go to the API service’s dashboard and revoke that Key, then generate a new one. Don’t think deleting that commit fixes it — Git history sticks around.

How to communicate with AI: Prompts and Markdown

This last one doesn’t look technical, but it directly affects whether your Vibe Coding succeeds.

You’ve probably had this happen: you say one thing to the AI and it builds something completely different from what you wanted. This usually isn’t because the AI is dumb — it’s because your description left too much room for interpretation.

This is where Markdown is your friend.

What’s Markdown? You can think of it as “enhanced plain text”. Originally plain text is just lines of text with no titles, no bold, no bullet points. Markdown lets you mark structure with simple symbols — # for titles, - for bullets, **text** for bold.

If you’ve used Notion, Discord, or ChatGPT, you’ve already encountered Markdown — these tools all support Markdown syntax. Making your Prompts structured helps the AI understand the hierarchy you want to express.

For example, if you only tell the AI:

The AI fills in lots of details on its own. But if you write it in Markdown:

Now the AI understands your needs more precisely, and the result will be closer to what you want.

For more advanced Prompt techniques — like mixing BDD (behavior-driven) and SDD (specification-driven) — I covered them in detail in A quick take on AI Vibe Coding’s mainstream methodologies — BDD and SDD. Beginners can come back to this once you’re more comfortable.

Note
If you want to understand what AI terms like LLM, Prompt, Token mean behind the scenes, also check out Heard of LLM, RAG, Prompt? A quick guide to common Generative AI terms.

Wrap up

Quick recap of what we covered:

Recommended learningImportanceWhy
Git and GitHubEssentialYour safety net when AI breaks things
Terminal command lineEssentialEntry point for most AI tools
VSCode editorEssentialWhere you read code and manually tweak
HTML/CSS/JS basicsRecommendedAt minimum to read what AI wrote
Frontend framework awarenessRecommendedKnow whether AI used React or Vue
UI Framework awarenessRecommendedKnow where the interface styles come from
Node.js and package managementRecommendedUsed in modern frontend projects
Reading error messagesEssentialKey to feeding errors back to AI
Environment variables and securityEssentialAvoid the giant API Key leak gotcha
Markdown and PromptsEssentialBasic skill for communicating with AI

From this table you can see, the fundamentals to learn for Vibe Coding aren’t actually as many as you’d think. They mostly revolve around three things: “how to interact with tools”, “how to communicate with AI”, and “how to save things when stuff breaks”.

I personally feel that even though Vibe Coding’s pitch is “no need to write code”, in practice you still need a basic understanding of development. Otherwise when the AI screws up, you’ll be completely out of options.

Of course, the things listed here aren’t meant to be learned all at once today and mastered tomorrow. Take your time — pick one or two that hurt the most for you and start there. Learn the rest as you encounter them.

Hope this gave you a better picture of the fundamentals to learn for Vibe Coding!

If after reading this you find there’s more to learn than just learning to write code directly, then maybe… you might consider just learning to write code directly (lol)
entry.summary: Lately a lot of friends have been asking me, "I want to use AI to build things too — where do I start?" And what they're really asking about is Vibe Coding. This post is about the fundamentals beginners should get down before diving into Vibe Coding — Git, command line, UI Frameworks, and so on. So you don't get halfway through and find you're stuck not knowing how to communicate with the AI.

entry.title: Decoding AI Spec Sheets: Token, Context Window, Compute — What Are These Numbers Actually About?
entry.content:
Intro

If you’re like me and you’ve been checking out a lot of AI product intro pages lately, you’ve probably seen this kind of stuff: “Supports 1M Token Context Window”, “100 billion parameters”, “Input only $3.00 per million Tokens”. Looks impressive, but what do these numbers actually mean?

I think a lot of people, when they first see these spec sheets, default to “bigger numbers = better, right?” But each number behind the scenes means something different — bigger isn’t always better. Once you understand these terms, you can actually judge which AI fits your needs, instead of just being led around by marketing numbers.

So this post breaks down all the jargon AI vendors love to use, so the next time you see one of these spec sheets, you won’t be lost.

Token: the smallest currency unit in the AI world

What is a Token?

If you’ve used ChatGPT or Claude, you’ve definitely heard the word “Token”, because everything from API pricing (an API is basically an interface that lets developers call AI services from code) to Context Window limits revolves around Tokens.

In short, a Token is the smallest unit a language model (the core tech behind ChatGPT, Claude, etc.) uses when processing text. You can think of it as the “basic currency” the AI uses when reading and producing text. Every time the AI reads your input or produces a response, the calculation is done in units of Tokens.

But a Token isn’t the same as “one character” or “one word” — how it gets split depends on the Tokenizer each model uses. For example, in English "darkness" might get split into two Tokens, "dark" and "ness", while "I" is one complete Token. So how does the Tokenizer decide how to split text?

Wait, doesn’t a computer only understand 0 and 1? Why do we need Tokens?

Before getting into Tokenizers, you might have a more basic question: “Doesn’t a computer only understand 0 and 1? Why doesn’t AI just process text in binary directly — why this extra Token concept?”

True, a computer’s most fundamental operation is 0 and 1, and all text in a computer ends up as a string of 0s and 1s. The English letter A is stored as 01000001 (8 bits of 0 and 1). The Chinese character 你 is more complex and needs 24 bits to represent. So if text can already be represented in 0s and 1s, why does a language model need an additional Token layer?

The reason is efficiency and semantics.

If you make a language model process text one character at a time, the length the model has to handle becomes really long. Take "Hello" — character by character it’s 5 units, which seems fine right? But take "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog" — character by character it’s 43 units. If we use Tokens via the splitting tech we’ll cover later, that same sentence might only be about 9-10 Tokens — several times shorter.

The difference becomes more pronounced with long text, because a language model’s compute requirements scale dramatically with length (roughly the square of the length). So 4x the length means 16x the compute — that’s just impractical.

Beyond efficiency, Tokens also capture more meaningful semantic units. The character d on its own doesn’t mean much, but the Token "dark" carries clear meaning — the model can directly learn that “dark” represents the concept of “darkness”, instead of having to slowly piece this concept together from d, a, r, k. So Tokens are essentially a balance point between “the binary world of computers” and “the semantic world of humans” — not so granular that efficiency tanks, not so coarse that the vocabulary list explodes.

How does a Tokenizer split text?

Most current language models use a splitting algorithm called BPE (full name: Byte Pair Encoding — no need to memorize that). GPT and Claude both use BPE or variations of it. The core logic isn’t hard to understand: it starts by splitting text into the smallest units (usually individual characters), then repeatedly merges the most commonly co-occurring “adjacent character pairs” into new Tokens, repeating until the vocabulary list (the “list of all Tokens this Tokenizer recognizes”) reaches a preset size.

For example, with the English word "hello", BPE starts by splitting into five characters:

Then because l and l frequently appear adjacent in the training data, they get merged into ll first:

Then if the combination he is also common, it gets merged too:

Eventually, after enough merging, hello itself might become a single Token in the vocabulary. Words like "darkness" might get split into "dark" and "ness" — two Tokens — because both parts are very common combinations in English. Interestingly, "brightness" also gets split into "bright" and "ness" — you’ll notice the suffix "ness" shares the same Token ID, which helps the model understand that “darkness” and “brightness” have some semantic relationship.

The final trained Tokenizer has a fixed vocabulary list, usually between 32,000 and 200,000 Tokens. For example, OpenAI’s vocabulary (called cl100k_base — 100k means about 100,000) contains roughly 100,000 Tokens, and all text input gets split against this list.

Why does Chinese consume more Tokens than English?

Here’s a real-world issue. Most language model Tokenizers are trained on text data that’s mostly English, so English Token efficiency is really high — about every 4 English letters maps to 1 Token, which works out to roughly 1-1.5 Tokens per English word.

Chinese is different. Because the Tokenizer mainly learned how to split text from English data, Chinese characters take up a relatively small share of the vocabulary. Most Chinese characters need 1.5 to 2 Tokens to represent, and rarer characters might need 3 Tokens. The reason is that the Tokenizer’s vocabulary size is limited (say 100,000 slots), but the common Chinese, Japanese, and Korean characters alone add up to tens of thousands — there’s no way every one gets its own slot, so many Chinese characters have to be represented as combinations of multiple Tokens.

A concrete comparison:

ContentTextApproximate Token count
English“Hello, how are you?”About 6 Tokens
Chinese“你好，你好嗎？”About 11 Tokens

Both express roughly the same meaning, but the Chinese version uses nearly twice as many Tokens. This means that under the same Context Window limit, Chinese users can fit less info in than English users. And if you’re calling the model via API, Chinese costs a bit more than English too.

Note
This problem has been gradually improving in recent years. OpenAI’s later o200k_base Tokenizer and various models’ multilingual optimizations have narrowed the gap in Token efficiency between Chinese and English — but the difference still exists.

Input Token vs Output Token

Another important concept is that Tokens are split into “Input Tokens” and “Output Tokens”.

Input Tokens are everything you send to the AI — your question, the documents you upload, the System Prompt (system instructions developers preset to tell the AI how to respond), conversation history, and so on. Add it all up and that’s your Input Token consumption.

Output Tokens are what the AI sends back to you — every Token produced consumes one Output Token.

Why split them out? Because generating text takes more compute than reading text, so Output Tokens usually cost several times more than Input Tokens. For mainstream models today, Output is typically priced 4-6x higher than Input. That’s why you’ll see API pricing tables list Input and Output separately.

In short, the longer your question and the more the AI replies, the more Tokens consumed and the higher the cost.

Context Window: the model’s “working memory” limit

What is Context Window?

If you’ve ever chatted with ChatGPT for a while and noticed it starts forgetting things you mentioned earlier, you’ve already personally experienced the Context Window limit.

Context Window is just the “maximum number of Tokens” a language model can handle in a single conversation. You can think of it as the AI’s work desk — bigger desk means more documents spread out for reference at once, but the desk has a finite size, and content beyond the limit gets pushed off or compressed.

Important to emphasize: Context Window includes everything — your System Prompt, conversation history, documents you upload, and the AI’s responses are all packed into the same Context Window. So when an AI vendor says “Supports 1M Token Context Window”, that doesn’t mean you can send a 1M-Token question and then get a 1M-Token answer. It means the entire conversation’s “input plus output total” can’t exceed that limit.

So how big is 1M Tokens really? Quick note on the units commonly seen in AI spec sheets: K stands for Thousand and M stands for Million. So 1M Tokens is 1 million Tokens, and 128K is 128,000 Tokens. 1 million Tokens converts to roughly 750,000 English words, or about 500,000-650,000 Chinese characters (since Chinese consumes more Tokens, as mentioned earlier) — basically several books worth of content.

How big are Context Windows these days?

If you check the intro pages from various AI vendors, you’ll see Context Windows ranging from hundreds of thousands to tens of millions of Tokens. This number gets updated every few months, so I won’t list specific model specs here — you can always check the latest numbers on each vendor’s official website.

But there’s one concept worth understanding: Context Window and “Maximum Output Tokens” are two different limits. Context Window refers to the total cap for the entire conversation (input + output), while Maximum Output Tokens refers to how many Tokens the model can produce in a single reply.

For example, say a model has a 1M Token Context Window but only 64K max output. That means it can “read” a lot (the entire Context Window can hold stuff), but it can only “write” up to 64K Tokens at a time — it can’t reply with an entire book in one shot. Some models even claim Context Windows of tens of millions, but their max output might only be a few thousand Tokens. So just looking at the Context Window number isn’t enough — you also need to pay attention to the max output limit.

Marketing numbers vs actual performance — different things

Here’s a really important concept: the advertised Context Window size and what the model can actually effectively handle are two different things.

According to various independent tests, most models start showing noticeable accuracy drops as they approach their Context Window limit. The model might miss important info you mentioned earlier, or its understanding of context can drift. Generally, the actually usable range is only about 60-70% of the advertised number.

This is similar to humans, actually. It’s like reading a 500-page book — by the time you’re at the end, you don’t remember the details from the early chapters that clearly. Language models have a similar problem. Research shows models remember the beginning and end of Context most strongly — content in the middle is what gets ignored most easily. This is called the “Lost in the Middle” phenomenon — basically, content stuffed in the middle is most easily ignored by the model.

So next time you see an AI vendor advertising “Supports 10M Tokens”, don’t immediately assume you can dump 10M Tokens in and expect a perfect response. In actual use, you still need to consider the effective handling range.

Note
A practical tip: if you need the AI to process a large amount of documents, instead of stuffing them all in at once, processing them in batches and summarizing before merging usually works better.

Compute: the engine behind AI

What is compute?

You’ve probably seen “compute”, “GPU”, and “NVIDIA” mentioned together in the news a lot. So what is compute?

In short, compute is “how many calculations a computer can do in a given time”, usually measured in FLOPS — Floating Point Operations Per Second, or “how many floating-point operations per second”. Floating-point operations means calculations involving numbers with decimal points, like 3.14 × 2.71. Almost all calculations inside an AI model are this type, so FLOPS has become the most common unit for measuring compute. The bigger this number, the more calculations per second this computer (or GPU — the processor chip on a graphics card, more on this later) can do.

Common compute units:

UnitFull nameOrder of magnitude
GFLOPSGiga FLOPS1 billion ops per second
TFLOPSTera FLOPS1 trillion ops per second
PFLOPSPeta FLOPS1,000 trillion ops per second
EFLOPSExa FLOPS1 million trillion ops per second

These numbers sound astronomical, but training a large language model needs astronomical compute on top of astronomical compute, which is why thousands of GPUs running in parallel are needed.

Training vs inference

In the AI world, compute usage splits into two main phases: Training and Inference.

Training is the process of getting the AI model to “learn knowledge” — like having a student read all the textbooks, papers, and web content, then “compressing” that knowledge into the model’s parameters. This process eats massive amounts of compute. Training a GPT-4-class model is estimated to need tens of thousands of GPUs running continuously for several months.

Inference is the model “applying its knowledge” after learning — every time you ask ChatGPT a question or have Claude write code, the calculations the model performs are inference. Compared to training, single inference calls need much less compute, but with millions of users worldwide using these AI services daily, the cumulative inference compute demand is also staggering.

A simple analogy: training is like spending years to develop a doctor, while inference is like that doctor seeing patients every day. Developing them requires huge time and resource investment, but once developed, each patient visit is relatively quick.

Why are GPUs better suited for AI than CPUs?

You might wonder why AI uses GPUs instead of regular CPUs. Quick intro to both:

  • CPU (Central Processing Unit): the “brain” inside your computer, handling all sorts of compute tasks — opening a browser, running programs, all on it
  • GPU (Graphics Processing Unit): originally a chip on graphics cards specifically for processing game graphics and 3D imagery

So why use a chip originally meant for gaming graphics to do AI? Because AI calculations are essentially massive amounts of numbers that need to be added and multiplied at the same time — and GPUs are naturally great at “doing tons of simple calculations simultaneously”. CPUs are better at “handling one really complex thing at a time”.

An analogy: CPUs are like a really smart math professor who can only do one problem at a time but does it fast and accurately. GPUs are like a thousand average calculators — each one only does simple addition, but with 1,000 working in parallel, the total speed far exceeds the professor. AI calculations happen to be that “simple but huge volume” kind of work, so GPUs became the most central hardware for AI.

The AI training market is currently almost monopolized by NVIDIA, with over 90% market share — one of the main reasons NVIDIA’s stock and market cap have grown so dramatically the past few years.

Parameters: the model’s “brain capacity”

What are parameters?

When you see news saying “this model has 70 billion parameters” or “GPT-5 supposedly has trillions of parameters”, what are these “parameters” actually?

In short, parameters are the “values” the language model learned during training. Every parameter is a number representing the model’s understanding of some language pattern. These numbers combined form the model’s ability to judge “what the next character should be”. Using a brain analogy, parameters are kind of like “synaptic connections” in the brain — more synapses, more complex info the brain can handle.

Note
If you want to dive deeper into the technical details of parameters and quantization, including how to pick a model based on your hardware, check out Want to run your own LLM? Understand parameters and quantization so you don’t pick the wrong model — it has more thorough explanations.

Are more parameters always better?

A lot of people intuitively think more parameters = stronger. There was a trend in this direction early on — the industry called it “Scaling Law”: the idea that as long as you make models bigger, feed more data, and throw enough compute at it, model capabilities keep improving. But by 2025-2026, people started noticing that simply increasing parameter count brings less and less improvement.

The more popular approach now is an architecture called MoE (Mixture of Experts). You can think of it like a big company with many departments specializing in different things, but each project only sends the most relevant departments to handle it — not the whole company. So with this architecture, the model’s “total parameter count” might reach trillions, but each query only activates a small subset of parameters. The actually-used parameters are called “Active Parameters”. For example, an MoE model claiming 2 trillion total parameters might only use 200 billion active parameters per query, keeping strong capabilities without burning through compute.

So when looking at parameter counts now, it’s more important to distinguish “total parameters” from “active parameters” — looking only at total parameters doesn’t accurately reflect a model’s actual capabilities anymore.

Why do some vendors not disclose parameter counts?

You might have noticed that OpenAI stopped publicly disclosing model parameter counts starting with GPT-4. Anthropic’s Claude series has never officially disclosed them either, and the same goes for Google’s Gemini.

The reason is mostly competitive considerations — disclosing parameter counts tells competitors your model architecture details, while also avoiding the market doing crude parameter-count-only comparisons. Most current industry estimates of parameter counts are unofficial numbers reverse-engineered from model behavior, inference speed, and pricing — so when you see these estimates, take them as reference only, no need to take them too seriously.

The “seasoning” for model inference: Temperature, Top-P, Top-K

Beyond the “hard specs” above, if you’ve used the API or some advanced AI tools, you might have run into Temperature, Top-P, Top-K. These aren’t model specs themselves — they’re “knobs” you can adjust at the time the model is generating a reply (the “inference” stage I mentioned), and they determine the style and randomness of the model’s reply.

Temperature

Temperature is the most common and most intuitive parameter. It controls the “creativity level” of the model’s reply, usually ranging from 0 to 2. I mentioned earlier that when a language model predicts the next character, it calculates the probability (likelihood) of each candidate. Temperature adjusts whether the model “takes risks” choosing characters with lower probabilities.

  • Lower Temperature (closer to 0): the model leans toward picking the highest-probability character, replies are more deterministic and consistent — good for code or factual questions
  • Higher Temperature (closer to 1 or above): the model is more willing to pick lower-probability characters, replies are more varied and creative — good for storytelling or brainstorming

In short, low Temperature = “conservative and stable”, high Temperature = “bold and risky”.

Top-P (nucleus sampling)

Top-P’s logic is “only pick from the most likely candidates until their combined probability reaches P%”. For example, Top-P set to 0.9 means the model first sorts all candidates from highest to lowest probability, then only considers the ones whose combined probability reaches 90% — the rest, which are very unlikely, are excluded.

The advantage of Top-P is that it adjusts dynamically. If the model is very confident about the next character, it might only consider 1-2 candidate Tokens, but if it’s uncertain, it considers more candidates — making sampling more flexible.

Top-K

Top-K is more straightforward: “only consider the top K Tokens by probability”. For example, Top-K set to 50 means no matter how the combined probabilities of those 50 Tokens stack up, the model fixes its choice to those 50.

Compared to Top-P’s dynamic adjustment, Top-K’s candidate count is fixed, which can be less flexible in some cases. In 2026 practice, most API vendors mainly expose Temperature and Top-P for users to adjust — Top-K is less commonly seen in commercial APIs.

Note
The general advice is to only adjust either Temperature or Top-P at a time — adjusting both can sometimes produce conflicting effects. If you’re not sure how to set them, the defaults are usually plenty good for most situations.

Token pricing: how do AI services charge?

After understanding the terms above, let’s wrap up with a practical question: how is AI API pricing actually structured?

Most mainstream AI APIs price per “million Tokens” (usually abbreviated as MTok), and Input Token vs Output Token pricing is calculated separately — Output is usually several times more expensive than Input.

Specific prices vary by vendor and model, and they change really fast, so I won’t list specific numbers here — you can check each vendor’s official pricing page for the latest. But a few concepts don’t change:

  1. Stronger models cost more: each vendor usually has a “flagship” tier and a “lightweight” tier. Flagship is the most capable but also the most expensive; lightweight is much cheaper but with limited capability — the price gap can be tens of times.
  2. Output is more expensive than Input: because generating text takes more compute than reading text, Output Token pricing is typically 4-6x Input.
  3. Prices keep dropping: AI API prices have been dropping rapidly — equivalent capabilities cost a fraction of what they did two years ago. Good news for both developers and regular users.

So a good usage strategy is to give simple tasks to cheap lightweight models, and reserve flagship models for genuinely complex tasks — striking a nice balance between cost and performance.

Wrap up

After reading this post, you shouldn’t be lost the next time you see an AI product spec sheet. Here’s a quick recap:

  • Token: the smallest unit AI uses for text, not the same as character count, Chinese consumes more Tokens than English
  • Context Window: the maximum total Tokens the model can process in one go — actual effective capacity is usually less than advertised, and pay attention to max output as a separate limit
  • Compute (FLOPS): a measure of computing power — training needs massive compute, inference is smaller per call but cumulatively staggering
  • Parameters: the count of learned numerical values in the model — distinguish total parameters from active parameters now
  • Temperature / Top-P: knobs for adjusting reply style — neither higher nor lower is universally better

Understanding the core concepts behind these terms matters more than memorizing specific numbers. Numbers update every few months, but concepts don’t change. Hopefully this post helps!
entry.summary: Every time you check out an AI product's intro page, you see things like Token, Context Window, hundreds of billions of parameters, $X per million Tokens — but what do these numbers actually mean? This post breaks down Token, Context Window, compute, parameters, Temperature, and all the other jargon AI vendors love to use, so you don't get lost in marketing numbers anymore.

entry.title: How Should You Install Software on a Mac? Homebrew vs Official Download vs curl
entry.content:
Intro

If you’ve just started using a Mac for development, you’ve probably noticed something: the same tool has several different install paths. Take Git — some say Xcode Command Line Tools is enough, others recommend Homebrew. Take Node.js — some say download from the official site, some say use nvm, some say use Homebrew.

This post sorts through the common Mac install methods and pairs them with concrete examples so you know which one fits which situation.

Why everyone recommends Homebrew

Before going through the various install methods, let me cover why most tutorials default to Homebrew.

Homebrew is the most-used package manager on macOS. If you haven’t installed it yet, you can follow my older post Make your Mac terminal pretty — the only guide you need (article in Mandarin). In short, it lets you manage software with terminal commands. What makes it better than just downloading from official sites?

Manage all your software in one place

Whether you install command-line tools or GUI apps through Homebrew, you can manage all of them with commands:

If you download tools from official sites instead, updates mean going back to those sites manually, and uninstalling means hunting down leftover files. After 20–30 tools, it gets really hard to manage.

Automatic dependency handling

Some tools require other tools to be installed first — like a package that needs openssl or cmake. If you’re installing by hand, you have to track down each dependency yourself. Homebrew handles them automatically — whatever’s missing, it pulls in.

Use Brewfile to back up and restore your whole machine

This is one of Homebrew’s most useful features. You can export everything you’ve installed via Homebrew as a Brewfile:

So when you switch to a new Mac or want to sync dev environments with a colleague, share the Brewfile and they’re one command away from having the same setup. You can’t do that by downloading from individual sites.

Formulae vs Cask

Homebrew has two install modes for two kinds of software:

brew install installs command-line tools. brew install --cask installs GUI apps. Using Cask gives you the same end result as downloading a .dmg, with the bonus that you can run brew upgrade --cask to update everything in one shot and brew uninstall --cask for a clean uninstall.

Other install methods

Beyond Homebrew, there are a few other approaches, each with their place.

Xcode Command Line Tools

If you’re not doing iOS development, you don’t need full Xcode — Xcode Command Line Tools is enough. It includes Git, make, clang, and the basic dev tools:

This is usually the first command you run on a new Mac, since many tools (Homebrew included) depend on it.

Official .dmg / .pkg downloads

Going to the software’s official site and downloading the installer. Simple and direct, but updates are on you, and uninstalls don’t always clean up — some software scatters config files around the system.

If the same software is available via Homebrew, prefer Homebrew. Much easier to manage.

curl install scripts

Some tools give you a curl one-liner:

This pattern usually shows up when the tool needs to modify your shell config (like .zshrc), which isn’t a great fit for Homebrew’s standard flow. Homebrew itself installs via curl.

Note
Before running a curl script, make sure the source is the official repo. You’re literally executing code from the internet on your machine.

Version managers

If you’re maintaining multiple projects that each need different language versions (project A on Node.js 18, project B on Node.js 20), you want a version manager — not a plain Homebrew install.

Common version managers:

ToolManagesInstall method
nvmNode.jscurl script
pyenvPythonHomebrew or curl script
rbenvRubyHomebrew
miseEverything (900+ tools)Homebrew or curl script

Of these, mise can manage Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, Java, etc., all at once — so you don’t have to install a separate version manager per language.

If you want to see how nvm works in practice, I wrote about it in Auto-switch Node.js version with nvm use when opening a project on Mac (article in Mandarin).

Real examples

Now let’s walk through a few common tools and the install method that fits each.

Git

Git comes with Xcode Command Line Tools, but the bundled version is usually older. If you want a newer version, just use Homebrew:

The Homebrew version auto-overrides the Xcode CLT one. Updates are just brew upgrade git.

Recommendation: Xcode CLT is fine for most users. If you care about version, use Homebrew.

Node.js

Node.js is the tool people get confused about the most:

Downloading the .pkg from the official site isn’t great because it installs at a system-level path, which makes switching versions or uninstalling painful.

Recommendation: Frontend or Node.js devs should use nvm or mise. If you only touch Node.js occasionally, Homebrew is enough.

Python

Mac ships with Python, but it’s an old version and not great for dev:

Recommendation: If you write Python regularly, use pyenv or mise. If something else just needs Python as a dependency, Homebrew is fine.

VS Code

VS Code is a GUI app — both methods get you the same result:

Or just download .dmg from the VS Code site.

Recommendation: If you’re already on Homebrew, use Cask for consistency.

Summary

Quick reference table:

What you’re installingRecommended method
Command-line tools (git, wget, jq)Homebrew
GUI apps (VS Code, Chrome)Homebrew Cask or official download
Languages needing multiple versions (Node.js, Python)Version manager (nvm, pyenv, mise)
Xcode, Mac-only appsMac App Store
Version managers themselves (nvm, mise)curl script or Homebrew

My personal new-Mac install order looks something like this:

  1. xcode-select --install (basic dev tools)
  2. Install Homebrew (the package management core)
  3. All command-line tools via Homebrew
  4. Node.js via nvm
  5. GUI apps via Homebrew Cask or official download depending on the situation

You don’t have to memorize every method. The rule of thumb is: if Homebrew can manage it, use Homebrew; if you need multiple versions, use a version manager. That alone covers most decisions.
entry.summary: If you've just started using a Mac for development, you've probably noticed there are several different ways to install the same tool — download a `.dmg` from the official site, install with Homebrew, run a `curl` install script, and tutorials sometimes recommend different approaches for the same tool. This post sorts through the common Mac install methods, pairs them with real examples (Git, Node.js, Python, VS Code), so you know which one to pick when.

entry.title: From 3 to 100: Passing isitagentready's AI Agent Readiness Test
entry.content:
Intro

If you run a tech blog like I do, you’re probably already familiar with SEO. I’ve written before about AEO (Answer Engine Optimization) (article in Mandarin) — basically how to get AI search engines to cite your content. But recently I came across something that goes a step further: Cloudflare’s isitagentready.com, a tool that scans your site and tells you, “Is your website ready for AI Agents to drop by?”

Out of curiosity I threw my own blog at it — and almost fell out of my chair when the results came back. Out of 12 items I only passed 3, with a sad little score of 3 out of 100. The Level it gave me? The lowest possible.

So this post walks through the whole process of getting from 3 all the way to 100. If you want to try the same thing on your own site, you can follow along.

What is Agent Readiness, and why should you care?

Before we start fixing anything, let’s talk about what this thing actually is.

Have you ever stopped to think about how ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and other AI tools actually “see” your website when they access it? They don’t have eyeballs like humans — they need standardized signals to figure out what’s on your site and what it can do.

Imagine you run a small shop. Human customers walk in, look at the sign, read the menu, done. But if your customer is an “AI customer,” it needs you to hang a machine-readable QR code or some kind of standardized label out front so it knows what you sell and how to order.

Agent Readiness is exactly that — does your website have those “signs for AI” properly hung up?

What is isitagentready.com actually testing?

The tool grades your site across four dimensions, totaling 12 items. Let me break down what each one is doing in plain English.

Category 1: Discoverability (can AI even find you?) — 3 items

This category checks whether AI can “discover” your site, like whether your shop has a sign out front:

  • robots.txt: a file that tells crawlers “here’s what you’re allowed to look at, and here’s what’s off-limits”
  • Sitemap: a map of your site that tells AI all the pages you have
  • Link headers: signposts placed inside your HTTP response that point AI toward more resources (API catalogs, RSS, etc.)

Category 2: Content (can AI understand what you have?) — 1 item

  • Markdown Negotiation: when an AI says, “hey, can you give me this in Markdown?”, can your site actually return Markdown instead of a pile of HTML tags?

Why does AI prefer Markdown? Because Markdown is clean — no CSS classes, no nested div soup. AI processes it faster and burns way fewer tokens.

Category 3: Bot Access Control (rules for crawlers) — 2 items

  • AI bot rules: does your robots.txt have specific rules for AI crawlers (like GPTBot, ClaudeBot)?
  • Content Signals: have you clearly told AI, “you can use my content for search, but not for training”?

Category 4: Protocol Discovery — 6 items

This category is more “advanced.” It’s mostly about hosting standardized JSON files on your site so AI Agents know which interaction methods you support:

  • API Catalog: publish your API directory
  • OAuth/OIDC: describes your authentication methods
  • OAuth Protected Resource: describes protected resources
  • MCP Server Card: basic info about your MCP server
  • Agent Skills index: which “skills” your site exposes to AI
  • WebMCP: expose tools to AI directly through JavaScript on the page

Honestly, these 6 don’t mean much for a pure blog — they’re more relevant for sites that actually offer API services. But hey, if we’re going for the perfect score, let’s go all the way.

My starting point: 3 out of 100

The very first scan looked like this:

  • ✅ robots.txt (already had one)
  • ✅ Sitemap (Hexo generates it automatically)
  • ✅ AI bot rules (set these up back when I wrote the AEO post)
  • ❌ The other 9 items: all failing

Alright, time to fix them one by one.

Fix #1: Content Signals

This is the easiest one. Just add a single line to your robots.txt. The line tells AI: “you’re welcome to use my content for search, just don’t use it to train your models.”

What the three values mean:

  • ai-train=no: don’t use my content to train AI
  • search=yes: feel free to index for AI search
  • ai-input=no: don’t pipe my content directly into AI as input material

If your robots.txt is hand-maintained, just slap it on the end. In my case, I use Hexo, and the robots.txt is auto-generated by a script — so I had to go modify the script that generates it.

Fix #2: Link Response Headers

This one checks whether your homepage’s HTTP response includes a Link header.

Quick refresher on HTTP headers: every time a browser (or AI Agent) requests data from your site, your site returns the HTML — plus some “extra info” attached. That extra info is the headers. The Link header is one of them, used to say, “hey, here are some related resources you might want to check out.”

Important note: this needs to be an HTTP header, not the <link> tag inside your HTML. Two different things.

If your site goes through Cloudflare (the orange cloud is on for your DNS), you can use Cloudflare’s Transform Rule to add it:

  1. Go to the Cloudflare Dashboard, pick your domain
  2. Go to Rules > click Create Rule in the top right > select Response Header Transform Rule
  3. Name the rule Agent Discovery Link Headers
  4. Set the condition: field “URI Path”, operator “equals”, value /
  5. Scroll down to the “Then…” section and choose “Add static”
  6. Header name: Link, value: whatever resources you want to point to (API catalog, Sitemap, RSS, etc.)

Once that’s saved, every time someone (or some AI) hits your homepage, Cloudflare will tack the Link header onto the response automatically.

Fix #3: Markdown Negotiation

This one wants your site to support “content negotiation” — meaning when an AI Agent requests a page with Accept: text/markdown in the header, your site should return Markdown instead of regular HTML.

The approach here is to use a Cloudflare Worker. Think of a Worker as a “middleman” — every request that goes through Cloudflare passes through the Worker first. The Worker can check whether this request is from an AI Agent (does it have Accept: text/markdown?), and if so, convert the HTML to Markdown before returning it.

I’ll cover this Worker together with the .well-known solution below, since they share the same Worker.

Fix #4: WebMCP

WebMCP is a newer standard that lets your webpage tell an AI Agent — via JavaScript — “hey, here are the things you can do on my site.”

Implementation is simple: add a snippet of JavaScript to your page that calls navigator.modelContext.provideContext() to register some “tools.”

For the blog, I registered two tools — one for searching articles, and one for fetching basic site info:

Drop this into your site’s HTML template and you’re done. Make sure it runs on page load — the scanner detects WebMCP tools at the moment the page loads.

Fix #5: The .well-known series

Now for the final five items. They all require placing JSON files under your site’s /.well-known/ path. /.well-known/ is a web standard for a public information directory, letting AI Agents discover what services and capabilities your site offers via fixed paths.

The files you need to host:

  • /.well-known/api-catalog - API catalog
  • /.well-known/openid-configuration - authentication info
  • /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource - protected resource info
  • /.well-known/mcp/server-card.json - MCP server info
  • /.well-known/agent-skills/index.json - Agent Skills index

You might be thinking, “I’m just a static blog — I don’t have an API or OAuth, why do I need any of this?” Fair. These items were originally designed for sites with backend services, and realistically no AI Agent is going to actually call your OAuth endpoint on a static blog. But the scanner only checks “do these files exist, and is the format correct?” — so if you want a perfect score, just throw them up there.

A heads up if you’re hosting on GitHub Pages: GitHub Pages can’t properly serve files under .well-known, even with .nojekyll in place. So we hand this off to a Cloudflare Worker too.

The Markdown Negotiation fix already needs a Worker, so we just bake the .well-known responses into the same one. The trick is to set up a lookup table inside the Worker, and when a request hits a .well-known path, return the matching JSON directly:

This single Worker solves both problems at once: serving .well-known files and handling Markdown content negotiation.

Worker deployment steps

  1. Go to the Cloudflare Dashboard, on the left sidebar select Workers & Pages
  2. Click Create > Create Worker
  3. Pick any name, e.g. agent-readiness
  4. Click “Deploy” to push the default code first
  5. After deploying, click “Edit Code” and paste your Worker script in
  6. Click “Deploy” in the top right

After deploying, you also need to set up routes so Cloudflare knows which requests should go through this Worker:

  1. Go back to your domain’s management page
  2. On the left, choose Workers Routes
  3. Add a new route, set the route to yourdomain.com/*, and pick the Worker you just created
  4. Save

You can also turn off the Worker’s workers.dev public URL — you only need it bound through the route, no need for a separate public entry point.

Note
You might be wondering if the free Cloudflare Worker plan’s 100,000 daily requests is enough. For a typical tech blog with a few hundred to a few thousand daily views, you’ll use less than 1% of the free quota. Don’t sweat it.

Final result: a perfect 100

After fixing everything, I rescanned, and:

  • Discoverability: 100 (3/3)
  • Content: 100 (1/1)
  • Bot Access Control: 100 (2/2)
  • API, Auth, MCP & Skill Discovery: 100 (6/6)
  • Total: 100, Level 5 Agent-Native 🎉

Quick recap of how each item was solved:

ItemHow it was fixed
robots.txtAlready there
SitemapAlready there
AI bot rulesAlready there
Content SignalsAdded a Content-Signal line to robots.txt
Link headersCloudflare Response Header Transform Rule
Markdown NegotiationCloudflare Worker
WebMCPA snippet of JavaScript in the HTML
.well-known (5 files)All handled by Cloudflare Worker

A few extra notes

  1. GitHub Pages doesn’t support the .well-known directory: even with .nojekyll, it doesn’t work. You’ll need a Cloudflare Worker to serve those files
  2. Markdown Negotiation needs a Worker: a static site on its own can’t do content negotiation, you need the Worker to do it

Wrapping up

The whole process really wasn’t that complex — most items were just “add a file” or “add a setting” and call it a day. The bulk of the time was actually spent stepping on landmines and debugging.

That said, honestly, some of these items (looking at you, OAuth) don’t have any real meaning for a pure blog — they’re just there for the points. But the Discoverability and Content items? Those are genuinely worth doing. The AI Agent ecosystem is only going to get more mature, and getting this foundation in place early means your content has a much better shot at being correctly accessed and cited by AI down the line — so yeah, worth it.
entry.summary: I ran my blog through Cloudflare's isitagentready.com and got a brutal 3/100. Here's how I fixed every single item, one by one, all the way to a perfect score — code, screenshots, and the gotchas I hit along the way.

entry.title: Migrating from Firebase Storage to Cloudflare R2 — the Full Move
entry.content:
Intro

A while back I wrote Cache Firebase Storage images with Cloudflare Workers, completely free. The idea was to put a Cloudflare CDN layer in front of Firebase Storage to cut down on bandwidth costs.

After running with that for a while I noticed a problem: a cache is still just a cache. On cache miss, traffic still hits Firebase Storage. And as of February 2026, Firebase forces all projects to upgrade to the Blaze (paid) plan — which means the free-tier umbrella is gone, and going past the free quota means you get billed. Worse, there’s no notification — the bill just quietly grows.

My blog runs two Firebase Storage buckets with around 4800 images total, billed separately. Every time I wanted to check the bill, I had to open two Firebase consoles. The whole thing felt fiddly enough that I decided to just rip the bandage off and move everything to Cloudflare R2.

Why Cloudflare R2?

Before deciding to move, I compared the costs:

Firebase Storage (Blaze)Cloudflare R2
Storage$0.026/GB/month$0.015/GB/month
Egress (download)$0.12 ~ $0.15/GB$0 (free)
Free tier5GB storage (specific region only)10GB storage + 10 million reads/month

The killer difference is egress. Firebase charges $0.12–$0.15 per GB downloaded; R2 has zero egress fees — no matter how many times your images get read, no charge.

Blog images are the textbook “write once, read many” workload. Every article view loads its images; if any post ever goes viral, Firebase’s bandwidth bill spikes immediately. R2 has none of that risk.

For my 4800 images at around 10GB, I’m comfortably inside R2’s free tier — meaning after the migration, monthly cost is $0.

Final architecture

After moving, the architecture looks like:

Pretty similar to the previous Workers-caching-Firebase setup. The only change is what’s behind the Worker — R2 instead of Firebase. Since R2 and Workers are both Cloudflare, integration is simpler and there’s no external network hop, so it’s faster too.

Migration steps

Walk-through of the whole process.

Step 1: Download all images from Firebase Storage

First, get all the images off Firebase Storage. 4800 images is not a “click each one” job, so I wrote a Node.js script.

You need a Firebase service account key to run it. Firebase Console → Project Settings → Service Accounts → Generate New Private Key gets you a JSON file.

Key points in this script:

  • 10 parallel downloads: not so fast that it gets rate-limited, not so slow you’re sitting around.
  • Resume support: progress is saved to .progress.json, so re-running after an interruption skips already-downloaded files.
  • Auto-retry: each file retries up to 3 times on failure.

After running, my numbers were 3920 files succeeded, 48 failed. But looking at the failure list, all the failures were paths ending in / like blogImg/Git/ — not actual files, just Firebase Storage’s placeholder objects for “folders”. So in practice, everything downloaded successfully, nothing to worry about.

Note
Downloading from Firebase Storage incurs egress charges. For 4800 images at ~10GB, that’s roughly $0.12/GB × 10GB = $1.2 USD, about 40 TWD. No need to panic.

Step 2: Upload to Cloudflare R2

Now upload to R2. R2 is S3-compatible, so I used the AWS SDK.

In the Cloudflare dashboard, create your R2 bucket, then go to R2 → Manage R2 API Tokens → Create API Token to get the Access Key ID and Secret Access Key.

Create a .env.r2:

Upload script:

There’s a gotcha here I’ll cover later.

Step 3: Set up the Cloudflare Worker

After uploading, you need a Worker to proxy image reads from R2. In the Cloudflare dashboard, create a Worker, then Settings → Bindings → add an R2 Bucket binding. Variable name BUCKET, select your bucket.

Worker code:

Compared to the old Worker that cached Firebase, the biggest change is the backend: fetch(firebaseUrl) is replaced by env.BUCKET.get(path), reading directly from R2 — no external network hops.

Step 4: Bulk-replace article URLs

Once the Worker’s deployed and images are loading correctly, the last step is rewriting all the old URLs across your articles.

The original Firebase Storage URL looks like:

Should become:

I wrote a script:

My blog had 289 files and 1436 URLs to replace, plus a second bucket with 154 files and 835 URLs — totaling 443 files and 2271 URLs, all done by the script.

Gotchas I hit

The migration went pretty smoothly overall, but I did hit some sharp edges. Logging them here in case you’re heading down the same path.

Gotcha 1: URL encoding

This one hurt the most.

Firebase Storage URLs encode path separators (/) as %2F, and Chinese characters and spaces are also percent-encoded. When replacing, I initially used decodeURIComponent on the whole path — which left spaces and Chinese characters directly in the URL:

Markdown’s image syntax ![](url) breaks on a literal space, so every image with Chinese or spaces in the path was busted.

The right approach is to convert only %2F to /, leave everything else encoded:

Gotcha 2: Upload path prefix

When uploading to R2, the key should be blogImg/Git/202107092329.png, but my prefix replacement didn’t quite match (./firebase-images/ vs firebase-images/), so the key ended up as firebase-images/blogImg/Git/202107092329.png — with an extra directory level.

You can patch around it in the Worker, but it’s ugly. Better to use a regex in the upload script:

Gotcha 3: Google Analytics tracking parameters

Some image URLs had a mysterious &_gl=1*5qrrwu*_ga*... tacked on. This is Google Analytics’s cross-domain tracking param that gets attached when you copy a URL from the Firebase Console — and it makes images fail to load.

Cleanup is trivial:

20 files and 55 URLs in my blog had this. Cleaned them all up and everything came back fine.

After the migration

Before/after comparison:

BeforeAfter
Storage2 Firebase Storage buckets1 Cloudflare R2 bucket
Management2 Firebase consoles1 Cloudflare dashboard
Egress fee$0.12/GB, no ceiling$0
Monthly costDepends on traffic, unpredictable$0 (within free tier)
Image domain2 different domainsUnified images.israynotarray.com
Traffic spike riskYes — bill jumpsNo, bandwidth is always free

The big win is no longer worrying about bandwidth costs. No matter how many people read which post or how many times the images get loaded, the cost is $0. I can finally sleep at night (lol).

Closing thoughts

The whole migration took about an afternoon, most of which was waiting for downloads and uploads. Once the scripts were running, it was just “wait for it to finish”.
