Most of the gotchas were URL encoding stuff, but once you remember the rule “only convert %2F, leave the rest alone”, you’ll mostly avoid the pitfalls.

If you’re also using Firebase Storage for images and you’re tired of looking at that bandwidth bill, moving to R2 is genuinely the most cost-effective option right now.
entry.summary: Earlier I wrote about caching Firebase Storage images with Cloudflare Workers, but in the end caching is just a layer in front — the images still live on Firebase and the bill still runs. So I ended up moving everything off the two Firebase Storage buckets onto Cloudflare R2 — nearly 4800 images, 2200+ article links handled in one shot. This is the full migration log, including the gotchas and the final architecture.

entry.title: Cache Firebase Storage Images with Cloudflare Workers — Completely Free Bandwidth Savings
entry.content:
Intro

If you’re using Firebase Storage to host blog images like I was, you’ve probably noticed bandwidth costs creeping up. Every time someone visits an article, the images load straight from Firebase Storage, and all of that bandwidth lands on your Firebase bill. The more articles and readers you have, the worse the bandwidth bill gets.

Is there a way to put a cache layer in front so images don’t get fetched from Firebase Storage every time? Yes — and completely free. This post walks through how to use Cloudflare Workers with a custom subdomain to cache Firebase Storage images, letting Cloudflare’s global CDN absorb most of the traffic.

How does this work?

Before getting into the implementation, let’s cover the principle. Otherwise you won’t know why it works.

In short, we’re going to put Cloudflare in front of Firebase Storage as a “middleman” that caches the images:

The first time someone requests an image, Cloudflare fetches it from Firebase Storage and stashes a copy at the edge. Every subsequent request goes straight from the edge — Firebase doesn’t get hit, which means no bandwidth charge on the Firebase side.

Is this really free?

Before we go any further, let me cover the cost — that’s probably what most people care about anyway:

ItemCost
Cloudflare accountFree
Cloudflare DNS hostingFree
Cloudflare WorkersFree (100,000 requests/day, no credit card)
Cloudflare CDN cached trafficFree (no bandwidth cap)
Custom domain for WorkerSupported on the free plan

All of it free. Cloudflare is one of the few CDNs that doesn’t bill on bandwidth, and people have run 5–9 TB/month on the free plan without trouble.

And once a request hits cache, it doesn’t go through the Worker at all — so it doesn’t consume your daily 100k Worker invocation quota either. For blog images, this is more than enough.

Note
The one requirement is that you own a domain and host its DNS with Cloudflare (which is also free).

Watch out for the paid traps

The Workers free plan doesn’t require a credit card, but the dashboard has features (Durable Objects, Workers AI, etc.) that might nudge you toward the $5/month paid plan. Don’t click any “upgrade” button — we only need basic Worker functionality, none of those advanced features.

Why a subdomain?

You might be thinking: “why not just use yourdomain.com/images/...?”

Simple — if your main domain is already running a blog or another project, the paths can collide easily. A subdomain (like images.yourdomain.com) is fully isolated and won’t interfere with anything on the main site. And the final image URL ends up looking like:

Way cleaner than the long Firebase Storage URL.

Let’s build it

Now that we’ve covered the principle and cost, on to the implementation.

Step 1: Host your domain DNS on Cloudflare

If your domain isn’t on Cloudflare yet:

  1. Sign up for a Cloudflare account (free).
  2. Click Add a site → enter your domain.
  3. Pick the Free plan.
  4. Cloudflare gives you two nameservers.
  5. At your domain registrar, change nameservers to the ones Cloudflare gave you.
  6. Back in Cloudflare, click confirm, wait for DNS to propagate (usually minutes to hours).

Note
If your domain’s already on Cloudflare, skip to Step 2.

Step 2: Create a Cloudflare Worker

  1. Cloudflare dashboard → left sidebar → Workers & Pages.
  2. Click Create → Create Worker.
  3. Pick “Start from Hello World!”.
  4. Give it a name, e.g. image-proxy.
  5. Click Deploy to create it, then Edit code.
  6. Delete the default code and paste this:

Note
Remember to replace YOUR_PROJECT.appspot.com with your actual Firebase project name, and allowedOrigins with your own domain.

  1. Click Save and Deploy.

What does this code do?

In case you’re wondering what’s actually happening — three things:

  1. Domain restriction: checks the request origin and only allows your own domain and local dev. Stops other people from hotlinking your image proxy.
  2. Reverse proxy: builds the Firebase Storage URL from the request path, fetches from Firebase.
  3. Cache headers: adds cache-control headers on the response, telling the Cloudflare CDN and the browser to cache the image for a year.

The localhost entry exists so local development still shows images. Since we’re using startsWith for matching, any port (4000, 3000, 8080…) works fine.

Step 3: Bind a subdomain

  1. In the Worker → Settings → Domains & Routes.
  2. Click Add → Custom Domain.
  3. Enter images.yourdomain.com.
  4. Cloudflare creates the DNS record for you, click confirm.

Step 4: Configure Firebase Storage Rules

Because our Worker requests Firebase Storage without a token, the images need to be publicly readable.

Firebase Console → Storage → Rules:

This only opens up blog2/ for public read — other paths are unaffected.

You might be worried about the security implications, but consider: these images were already public via blog articles — anyone could see them. The original token was sitting in your HTML for anyone who opens devtools to grab anyway. Switching to public read doesn’t actually add any new risk.

Step 5: Verify

Open a browser and type in:

If you see the image, you’re good.

Updating image links

Now you need to replace all the Firebase Storage image URLs in your blog with the new format.

Original looks like:

Becomes:

The rules:

  1. Swap the Firebase domain for images.yourdomain.com.
  2. Drop the ?alt=media&token=xxx.
  3. Convert %2F to /.

If you have lots of articles, doing it by hand isn’t realistic. Bulk replace with sed:

Note
Step 2 needs to run multiple times because sed only replaces one %2F per pass — the loop ensures all of them get converted. Also, if you have images from multiple Firebase projects, double-check you’re only replacing URLs from the target project so you don’t accidentally touch other projects’ image URLs.

Important: use / instead of %2F in URL paths

One detail worth highlighting.

Firebase Storage’s URL format uses %2F to separate paths (because the whole file path is treated as a single URL segment), but on our own subdomain, you must use a regular /:

Why? Because Cloudflare doesn’t handle %2F correctly in this context — you’ll get 404s. The Worker internally converts / back to Firebase’s %2F format (via encodeURIComponent), so on your end you just use normal paths and don’t need to worry about encoding.

Getting URLs for new images

How do you get URLs for new images going forward? The rule is just:

For example, if you upload an image to blog2/ai/my-post/screenshot.png in Firebase Storage, the URL is:

No more tokens. Just concatenate the path. Way more convenient.

How the caching works

Last bit: let’s cover how the caching actually flows, so you can feel more confident about the setup.

The Worker sets two key headers:

These set up two cache layers.

Layer 1: Cloudflare CDN edge cache

CDN-Cache-Control tells Cloudflare’s edge nodes: “cache this for a year”. On the first request, Cloudflare fetches from Firebase and stores a copy at the edge closest to the user. After that, anyone requesting the same image gets it straight from the edge — no Worker invocation, no Firebase hit.

Layer 2: Browser local cache

Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable tells the browser: “cache this locally for a year, and it won’t change”. So when the same user visits the same post again, the browser uses its local copy — doesn’t even hit Cloudflare.

Overall flow

First request (cold cache):

Second request (CDN hit):

Same user revisiting (browser cache hit):

Since blog images don’t change after upload, setting a 1-year cache with immutable is totally reasonable.

Closing thoughts

With Cloudflare Workers + CDN caching, you can dramatically cut Firebase Storage bandwidth — for $0. Setup doesn’t take much time, but the savings are long-term.

Recap of the benefits:

  • Completely free: no credit card, no paid plan needed.
  • Global acceleration: Cloudflare has 300+ edge nodes worldwide; images come from the closest one.
  • Reduced bandwidth: cache hits never touch Firebase, bandwidth goes to zero.
  • Domain restriction: only your own site can access — stops hotlinking.
  • Shorter URLs: from long Firebase URLs to clean subdomain paths.

If you’re also using Firebase Storage for images, this approach is worth trying.
entry.summary: If you're using Firebase Storage to host blog images like I was, you've probably noticed bandwidth costs creeping up — every article view pulls the images straight from Firebase Storage, and all of it lands on your Firebase bill. This post walks through how to use Cloudflare Workers plus a custom subdomain to cache those images. The whole setup is free and doesn't require a credit card.

entry.title: Want to Run Your Own LLM? Understanding Model Parameters and Quantization
entry.content:
Intro

If you’ve ever tried downloading an AI model to run on your own machine, you’ve probably hit Hugging Face and seen 7B, Q4_K_M, GGUF — a wall of codes with no clue which one your computer can handle. This post unpacks what those codes actually mean and how to pick a model that fits your hardware.

Note
If you don’t know how to run a model locally yet, check out Setting Up Your Own AI Language Model Lab With LM Studio (article in Mandarin) first — it covers everything from installation to usage.

What does the B after the model name mean?

When you’re browsing models, you’ll often see a number followed by B after the model name — 4B, 7B, 70B. That B stands for Billion, and it’s how many parameters (Parameters) the model has.

So what’s a parameter? Simple: parameters are the numerical values the model learned from massive amounts of data during training. If you want a more everyday analogy, think of parameters as the books a person has read — someone who has read tens of thousands of books can answer way more questions than someone who has read a few thousand, right? It’s the same idea for parameters: 7B means 7 billion parameters, 70B means 70 billion. More parameters means the model has learned more.

I touched briefly on B/M/T notation in Setting Up Your Own AI Language Model Lab With LM Studio (article in Mandarin) — this post goes deeper into how parameter count translates to actual usage.

Bigger parameter count is always better, right?

You might think: “Why not just grab the model with the biggest number?”

It’s not that simple. More parameters means more hardware demand — your machine has to be beefy enough to actually run it. And more parameters doesn’t automatically mean smarter. A well-trained 14B model can outperform a mediocre 70B one. So parameter count is just one signal, not the whole story.

What kind of parameter range fits what kind of task? Here’s a rough breakdown:

ParametersTierSuited for
1B - 3BLightweightSimple chat, summarization, translation — phones and low-end devices
4B - 9BEntryCoding assist, Q&A, general chat — the starting point for most people
13B - 14BMidNoticeably better reasoning, can handle more complex problems
27B - 32BAdvancedMulti-step reasoning, domain-specific Q&A
70B+High-endApproaches cloud-service quality, hardware bar is high

Looking at the table — for most people, 4B - 9B is already plenty. No need to jump straight to 70B.

Will my computer actually run it?

Once you know the parameter count, the next very practical question is: how much memory do I actually need?

Easy rule: with no compression, every billion parameters takes about 2 GB of memory. So a 7B model needs around 14 GB, and a 70B model needs about 140 GB.

Seeing that 140 GB number, you might already be sweating — “My machine only has 16 GB of RAM, so I can only run the smallest models?”

Hold on — that’s exactly what quantization solves. We’ll get to it shortly. First, let’s look at what else is hiding in those model names.

Decoding every part of a model name

Say you spot this model on Hugging Face:

It looks dense, but each segment means something specific:

PartMeaning
bartowskiUploader (a well-known community quantizer)
MetaDeveloper (Meta AI)
LlamaModel family name
3.1Version number
8B8 billion parameters
InstructInstruction-tuned — follows your instructions
GGUFFile format (for running locally)

So what that whole string is telling you: a Meta Llama 3.1 model, repackaged by bartowski, 8B parameters, instruction-tuned, stored in GGUF format.

What do tags like Instruct, Base, Chat mean?

Model names often carry tags that signal what kind of training the model went through. The most common ones:

  • Base: the raw model — like a student who has read a lot of books but hasn’t learned how to converse yet. Not great for chatting directly
  • Instruct / IT: instruction-tuned — follows your instructions and replies accordingly. This is what most people want
  • Chat: optimised for multi-turn conversation
  • Coder: specialised for code generation
  • Distill: distilled from a larger model — keeps most of the capability but is smaller

Personally — if you just want to chat locally or have AI help with everyday tasks, see Instruct, pick it.

Note
The LM Studio post covers the basic concept of Distill vs. Instruct — feel free to flip back if you’re curious.

What’s MoE? Don’t panic when you see A22B

A lot of recent models include the MoE tag, like:

You see 235B and immediately think: “235 billion parameters? My machine has zero chance, right?”

Hold on — there’s an A22B after it. MoE stands for Mixture of Experts. What does that mean? Simply put, the model contains many “experts”, but it doesn’t activate all of them for every question — it only picks a few relevant ones to handle the task.

So 235B is the total parameter count across all experts (235 billion), and A22B is the Active parameter count — only 22 billion actually run for any given query. In other words, this model has the knowledge breadth of 235B parameters, but the runtime cost of a 22B-class model.

That’s why MoE has been getting popular — it makes giant models that used to be exclusively in the cloud actually runnable on consumer hardware.

Note
One catch: MoE models still need disk space for all parameters — the file size doesn’t shrink because only some experts are active. The savings are at runtime in memory and compute.

What is quantization? It’s how your machine runs big models

Earlier I mentioned that an uncompressed 7B model needs 14 GB of memory — still too much for most people. That’s where quantization comes in.

So what’s quantization? Think of it like photo resolution. A 50-megapixel photo is the sharpest, but the file is huge. If you compress it down to 12 megapixels, you basically can’t tell the difference on your phone screen, but the file is way smaller. Quantization works similarly — it lowers the precision of each parameter, shrinking the file and the memory footprint.

What do Q4, Q8 mean?

When you download a model, you’ll see codes like Q4_K_M and Q8_0. The number is the quantization precision — smaller means more compression and smaller files, but with some quality loss.

Using a 7B model as the example:

Quantization7B model sizeQuality impact
FP16 (no compression)About 14 GBNone
Q8_0 (8-bit)About 7 GBAlmost imperceptible
Q5_K_M (5-bit)About 4.8 GBVery minor
Q4_K_M (4-bit)About 3.8 GBMinor — most people’s pick
Q3_K_M (3-bit)About 3 GBStarts to feel noticeable
Q2_K (2-bit)About 2.5 GBClearly degraded — not recommended

Look at that table — the same 7B model goes from 14 GB to 3.8 GB at Q4_K_M, so an 8 GB machine can handle it. And Q4 quality loss is genuinely small — for everyday chat you won’t really feel it.

So what about K_S, K_M, K_L?

Quantization codes often have K_S, K_M, or K_L suffixes, which describe the compression strategy:

  • K_L (Large): keeps the most detail, biggest file
  • K_M (Medium): balanced — the most commonly recommended option
  • K_S (Small): most compressed, smallest file, fastest

The K refers to K-Quants, which is a smarter form of compression. Instead of squashing everything to the same precision, it figures out which parameters matter more and keeps higher precision for those, only compressing the less important ones harder. So overall quality is much better than naive compression.

Personally — for most situations, Q4_K_M is a great starting point.

How do I pick between GGUF, GPTQ, and AWQ?

Beyond quantization level, you’ll also see different file formats. Here are the three most common:

GGUF is the dominant format for running models locally — Ollama, LM Studio, and llama.cpp all use it. The big upside is that it runs on both CPU and GPU and isn’t picky about hardware. For most people, just pick GGUF.

GPTQ and AWQ are NVIDIA-GPU-only formats. They preserve quality well, but you have to have an NVIDIA card. No NVIDIA GPU? Skip them.

So if you’re not sure what to pick, just remember one combo: GGUF + Q4_K_M. It works for most situations.

Picking a model based on your hardware

With those concepts down, the practical question becomes: based on the hardware I have, what should I download?

8 GB RAM (no discrete GPU)

This tier is limited but still usable. Aim for 3B - 4B models with Q4_K_M, like Phi-4 Mini (3.8B) or Llama 3.2-3B — files are around 2 - 3 GB. Simple chat and summarization are fine, though inference will be slow.

16 GB RAM or 8 GB VRAM

This is the sweet spot for most people right now. You can run 7B - 8B models with Q4_K_M — Llama 3.1-8B or Qwen 2.5-7B — file sizes around 4 - 6 GB. Coding assist, Q&A, and translation all work well.

32 GB RAM or 16 - 24 GB VRAM

If you have an RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM) or 32 GB+ RAM, your options open up a lot. 30B-class models can use Q5_K_M or even Q6_K, and 70B can run at Q4_K_M. Quality is approaching cloud-service territory.

Apple Silicon Mac users

If you’re on a Mac, here’s some good news. Apple Silicon uses Unified Memory — system RAM can be used directly as GPU memory, which is a huge advantage for running LLMs.

For example, a 36 GB MacBook Pro can run 30B-class models (Q4 - Q8). A similarly priced Windows laptop, where system RAM and VRAM are separate, would have to fall back to CPU-only inference and be much slower.

Mac configMemoryModels it can run
M-series base8 - 16 GB3B - 7B (Q4)
M-series Pro18 - 36 GB13B - 30B (Q4 - Q8)
M-series Max64 - 96 GB70B (Q4) or 30B (near-lossless)
M-series Ultra128 GB+100B+ MoE models

A practical rule of thumb

A general guideline:

Rather than picking a big model and squeezing it into very low quantization, pick a slightly smaller model at higher quantization.

What does that mean? If your machine’s memory is limited, an 8B model at Q5_K_M will usually outperform a 13B model crushed down to Q3_K_M. Over-compression strips too much capability away.

Where to download models

Now that you know how to pick, where do you download them?

Hugging Face

Think of it as the GitHub of AI models — it’s the largest model hub right now. Beyond original models, the community uploads pre-quantized versions. When looking for GGUF models, recognised quantizers like bartowski and hugging-quants are reliable bets.

Ollama

If you don’t want to mess with details, Ollama is the easiest option. Open your terminal and run a single command to download and start a model:

It auto-downloads the model, picks a quantization level appropriate for your hardware, and drops you straight into a chat — no file picking required.

LM Studio

If you prefer a GUI, LM Studio lets you search, download, and load models entirely inside an app — no command line involved.

Note
For a full LM Studio walkthrough, see Setting Up Your Own AI Language Model Lab With LM Studio (article in Mandarin).

Popular open-source models in 2026

To wrap up, here are some of the more popular open-source models right now, so you have a starting point.

If this is your first time, the 7B - 14B range is a good place to begin:

ModelDeveloperParametersHighlights
Llama 3.1 8BMeta8BHighly versatile, strong community ecosystem
Qwen 2.5 7BAlibaba7BSolid Chinese performance, good multilingual support
Phi-4Microsoft14BReasoning above its weight class, MIT license
Gemma 3 9BGoogle9BMultimodal support, balanced overall

If your hardware is stronger, take on the 27B - 70B range:

ModelDeveloperParametersHighlights
Gemma 4 27BGoogle27B (MoE, 4B active)Fast, lower memory needs than peers
Qwen 2.5 32BAlibaba32BHigh quality, excellent Chinese performance
Llama 3.3 70BMeta70BHigher hardware bar, near cloud-service quality
DeepSeek-R1 distilledDeepSeek7B - 70BStrong reasoning, multiple sizes available

And on the MoE architecture side mentioned earlier, current standouts include:

ModelDeveloperTotal / Active paramsHighlights
Qwen3 235B-A22BAlibaba235B / 22BBig knowledge base, relatively low resource needs
DeepSeek-V3DeepSeek671B / 37BOne of the open-source ceilings right now
Llama 4 ScoutMeta109B / 17BSupports an extreme 10M token context

This space moves fast — new models drop almost every month, so these picks will shift over time. If you want to track the latest, watch Hugging Face’s Open LLM Leaderboard for benchmark rankings across models.

Wrapping up

Quick recap:

  • The B in model names means billions of parameters — bigger means more knowledge, but heavier hardware demands
  • Pick the Instruct variant when downloading; Base is for researchers
  • For MoE models with an A-prefixed number, the active parameter count is much smaller than the total — way easier on resources
  • Quantization compresses models; Q4_K_M is the best starting point for most people
  • For file format, GGUF is the safe pick — runs on both CPU and GPU
  • Pick a model that fits cleanly in your memory rather than cramming a giant model down to extreme quantization
  • Mac users have a natural edge for local inference thanks to Unified Memory

Hope this gives you a clearer picture of LLM parameters and quantization — next time you’re picking a model on Hugging Face, those codes won’t feel like alphabet soup anymore.
entry.summary: If you've ever tried downloading an AI model to run on your own machine, you've probably hit Hugging Face and seen 7B, Q4_K_M, GGUF — a wall of codes with no clue which one your computer can handle. This post unpacks what those codes actually mean and how to pick a model that fits your hardware.

entry.title: AI Agent Isn't One Thing Anymore — Understanding Coding Agents and Other AI Agent Types
entry.content:
Intro

A while back I wrote Have you heard of LLM, RAG, and Prompt? A walkthrough of common generative AI terms (article in Mandarin), where I introduced AI Agent as a catch-all term — a system that can understand a task, break it into steps, and use tools to finish the job.

But here in 2026, you keep seeing more specific terms popping up — Coding Agent, Research Agent, Browser Agent. AI Agent isn’t a fuzzy umbrella term anymore. So this post breaks down what these subcategories actually mean and how they differ from each other.

A quick recap of AI Agent

Before getting into subcategories, let me quickly recap what an AI Agent is.

The current industry consensus, in plain terms, is something like this:

A system where an LLM uses tools in a loop to achieve a goal.

A few things in that sentence matter:

  • The LLM makes the decisions: You don’t hardcode the flow — the LLM itself decides what to do next
  • It uses tools: It can read files, call APIs, browse the web, run commands, and so on
  • It runs in a loop: Take an action, observe the result, decide the next step, take another action — repeat until the task is done
  • It’s goal-oriented: There’s a clear job to finish, not aimless chatting

There’s an important distinction here. In their Building Effective Agents post, Anthropic separates Workflow from Agent. A Workflow is an LLM and tools running through a flow you wrote ahead of time — every step is something you defined. An Agent is the LLM dynamically choosing which tool to use and how, with the LLM in control of the whole process.

To put it in everyday terms — a Workflow is like hiring a part-timer to follow an SOP, where every step is spelled out. An Agent is more like hiring an experienced employee: you tell them the goal, and they figure out how to get there.

The main categories of AI Agent

Through 2025 and into 2026, specialized AI Agent tools have exploded, and the industry has started carving out subtypes. There’s no single agreed-upon taxonomy, but for the sake of communication, here are the common categories grouped by what these tools are aimed at:

CategoryDescriptionRepresentative tools
Coding AgentAgent specialized for software developmentClaude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Devin
Research AgentGathers data, cross-references, produces reportsGoogle Deep Research, Perplexity
Browser AgentAutomates web pages, fills forms, completes online tasksOpenAI Operator, Google Project Mariner

These categories aren’t strictly separate. Plenty of tools span multiple categories. Take Claude Code — it’s primarily a Coding Agent, but it can absolutely search the web if you ask it to. The point of categorizing isn’t to draw hard boundaries, it’s to tell you where each tool’s home turf is.

There’s also a trend worth mentioning: Multi-Agent (multi-agent collaboration). The idea is to compose different types of Agents together — a Research Agent gathers data, a Coding Agent implements, an analysis Agent verifies the result. It’s a bit like microservices — each Agent focuses on one thing, and they coordinate through some orchestration layer.

What is a Coding Agent?

A Coding Agent is an AI Agent specialized for software development. Its toolset and workflow are designed around how software actually gets built.

MIT’s Missing Semester course added an Agentic Coding lecture in 2026 that defines a Coding Agent like this:

A conversational AI model equipped with tools to read and write files, search the web, and execute terminal commands.

On the surface that sounds a lot like a regular AI Agent, right? The difference is that a Coding Agent’s entire design is built around the software development loop. Here’s a side-by-side:

AspectRegular AI AgentCoding Agent
DomainGeneral tasksSoftware development
ToolsetGeneral tools (search, APIs, etc.)Dev tools (filesystem, Git, terminal, test framework, Linter)
Feedback loopDepends on the taskWrite code → run tests → read errors → fix → run tests again
OutputText, decisions, actionsCode changes, commits, Pull Requests
Context understandingDepends on the taskNeeds to grasp the whole Codebase structure, dependencies, and type system
VerificationDepends on the domainTests, compilation, and lint can verify automatically

That last row is actually a huge advantage Coding Agents have over other types. Software development comes with objective verification baked in — whether the code runs, whether tests pass, whether there are syntax errors — these are all binary outcomes that don’t need a human to judge. That lets a Coding Agent keep trying and self-correcting without anyone stepping in. Other domains usually don’t have such a clean automated verification path.

Coding Agent vs Code Assistant

There’s another thing that’s easy to mix up: a Coding Agent is not the same as a Code Assistant.

The AI coding tools we used to use were mostly at the Code Assistant level. The original GitHub Copilot is the classic example. A Code Assistant does this — while you’re typing code, it suggests the next line or the next function. You’re still the main operator, it’s just helping along the way.

A Coding Agent, on the other hand, takes a goal from you and plans which files to change, makes the changes, runs the tests, and fixes errors on its own. You basically just review the final result.

To put it in everyday terms — a Code Assistant is like having a co-pilot in the passenger seat helping you read the road, but your hands are still on the wheel. A Coding Agent is more like autopilot — you give it a destination, it drives, and you just keep an eye on it to make sure things are fine.

These days many tools do both. GitHub Copilot started as a Code Assistant, but after adding Agent Mode and Coding Agent it became both. Cursor is similar — Tab autocomplete is Assistant-level, but Agent Mode is Coding Agent-level.

What Coding Agents are out there?

The main Coding Agents on the market right now look something like this:

Claude Code

Anthropic’s CLI dev tool — it runs straight in your terminal. It can understand the whole Codebase structure, read files, edit files, run commands, run tests on its own, and supports advanced features like Hooks, Skills, and MCP Servers. Personally, Claude Code is the Coding Agent I use the most.

Note
If you want a deeper dive into Claude Code, check out Want to develop with Claude Code? This post takes you from beginner to advanced (article in Mandarin).

GitHub Copilot Coding Agent

GitHub’s cloud-based Coding Agent that runs inside the GitHub Actions environment. You assign it to an Issue, and it spins up a branch in the background, writes code, runs tests, and opens a PR when it’s done. This is different from Copilot’s Agent Mode — Agent Mode runs inside VSCode, the Coding Agent runs in the cloud.

Cursor Agent Mode

Cursor’s Agent Mode can search the entire Codebase, edit across files, and run terminal commands. In 2026, Cursor also launched Background Agents — they clone your Repo to the cloud and work on isolated branches, so you can run multiple of them in parallel.

Devin

Cognition’s fully autonomous Coding Agent — the most extreme end of the automation spectrum. You give it a task and it runs the whole thing in a cloud environment from start to finish — coding, debugging, deploying. It’s a good fit for well-defined tasks that don’t need a lot of back-and-forth.

OpenAI Codex

OpenAI’s cloud-based Coding Agent that can run dev tasks asynchronously in the background. You give it an instruction and it finishes in a sandbox environment, then hands you the result for review.

Others

There’s also a handful of open-source or more niche tools, like Aider (terminal tool), Cline (VSCode extension), and OpenHands (self-hostable platform).

Note
If you want a full comparison of these tools, check out my earlier post How to pick AI Coding tools in 2026? Full comparison of GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code, Kiro, and Antigravity (article in Mandarin).

The core workflow of a Coding Agent

No matter which Coding Agent you pick, the core workflow is pretty much the same:

  1. Understand the task: You tell it what you want, it reads the relevant code to figure out the current state
  2. Plan an approach: Decide which files to change, how, and in what order
  3. Make the changes: Start editing — could be one file, could be a dozen
  4. Verify the result: Run tests, lint, compile, see what breaks
  5. Self-correct: If a test fails, read the error and try again
  6. Deliver: Commit, open a PR, or just leave the changes in your working directory

Coding Agents work because software development has clear verification (tests, compilation), which lets the Agent keep iterating in this loop until it gets the right result.

Wrap-up

Quick recap of the main points:

TermDescription
AI AgentThe umbrella term — a system where an LLM uses tools in a loop to achieve a goal
Coding AgentA subtype of AI Agent specialized for software development — can read/write files, operate Git, run tests
Code AssistantHelps you write code (autocomplete, suggestions). You’re still the main operator
Research AgentGathers data, cross-references, produces research reports
Browser AgentAutomates web pages and completes online tasks
Multi-AgentMultiple Agents collaborating on more complex tasks

The short version — AI Agent is still the umbrella term, but as tools multiply and applications widen, the industry has started carving out more specific names for different domains. For us as engineers, the one we’ll bump into most is the Coding Agent — those AI dev tools that can read code, edit code, run tests, and open PRs on their own.

Hope this gave you a clearer picture of how these terms differ.
entry.summary: Back when I wrote about AI terminology, AI Agent was used as a catch-all term for systems that can understand a task on their own, break it into steps, and use tools to get things done. But here in 2026, the term AI Agent has started to splinter into more specific subcategories — and the most mature one is the Coding Agent (some people call it Code Agent). This post walks through what these categories actually mean and how they differ.

entry.title: Is Your Claude Code Context Getting Eaten Alive? AGENTS.md and the Complete Context Management Guide
entry.content:
Intro

This post is about Claude Code’s Context management problem. A lot of people install a bunch of Skills, Plugins, and MCP Servers, then write a giant CLAUDE.md — and before they’ve even started working, half their Context is already gone. We’ll also cover AGENTS.md (a cross-tool universal standard) and how to make sure your Context goes where it actually matters.

Note
If you’re not familiar with Claude Code’s basic operations yet, check out Want to develop with Claude Code? This guide takes you from beginner to advanced (article in Mandarin) first. If you want to learn more about writing CLAUDE.md, see CLAUDE.md and Rules for AI writing tips (article in Mandarin).

A quick intro to the Context Window

Before we get into management, let me recap what the Context Window is.

Whether you’re using Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, or any other AI dev tool, there’s a Context Window concept behind all of them. You can think of it as the AI’s short-term memory. Everything the AI needs for this conversation has to fit inside this space — system instructions, rule files, conversation history, file contents it’s read, command execution results, and so on. The fuller the Context, the worse the AI performs. This concept applies to any AI dev tool.

This post uses Claude Code as the example, but the concepts apply regardless of which tool you use. The Context Window sizes for Claude Code right now are:

  • Standard: 200,000 tokens
  • Extended (Opus 4.6 / Sonnet 4.6): 1,000,000 tokens

But the space you actually have to work with is way less than you’d think, because Claude Code has already stuffed a bunch of stuff in there before you even start typing. The System Prompt alone takes several thousand Tokens, plus tool definitions, CLAUDE.md, Auto Memory, Skill descriptions, MCP tool names, environment info — for a project with a lot loaded up, you’re already 10K+ Tokens in before you’ve started working.

You can type /context in Claude Code to see your current Token usage. It uses a colored grid to show how much each item takes up, plus optimization suggestions.

And the Context Window doesn’t have to be totally full to cause problems. From my own experience, around 60% in the AI’s attention starts to drop — it might ignore stuff in your rule files, or forget things mentioned earlier in the conversation. When Context is about to fill up, Claude Code triggers Auto Compaction, which compresses prior conversation history into a summary to free up space. But Auto Compaction is actually pretty unreliable and easily loses important info, so don’t rely on auto-compaction. I’ll cover the right approach later.

The culprits eating your Context

Now that we understand Context, let’s talk about what’s most likely silently devouring your Context.

CLAUDE.md is too long

A lot of people stuff entire project docs, API docs, and detailed architecture explanations into CLAUDE.md, ending up with a several-hundred-line file. The problem is, CLAUDE.md is loaded every conversation — whether you need it for that conversation or not, it sits there occupying your Context.

For me personally, I’d suggest keeping CLAUDE.md under 200 lines and only including stuff Claude can’t infer from the code itself — build/test commands, team Code Style conventions (if they differ from defaults), important architectural decisions, common gotchas, and so on. If some rules only apply to specific directories, you can use .claude/rules/ with paths to scope them, so they only load when Claude accesses the relevant files.

I covered this in more detail in CLAUDE.md and Rules for AI writing tips (article in Mandarin).

Too many MCP Servers

Each MCP Server has a bunch of tool definitions. If Tool Search isn’t enabled, all the tools’ full definitions get loaded into Context at startup — the more Servers you install, the more Tokens get eaten up.

Good news: Claude Code has Tool Search (also called Deferred Tool Loading) enabled by default. This mechanism only loads MCP tool names initially — full definitions only get loaded when Claude actually needs to use a particular tool. This significantly reduces MCP Token consumption at startup. You can use /mcp to manage MCP Server connection states — disconnect Servers you don’t need right now to save a lot of Context.

Too many Skills and Plugins installed

Skills load similarly to MCP — only names and descriptions are loaded at startup, with full content only loading when you actually invoke a Skill. But Skill descriptions have a total cap (1% of Context Window by default), so installing too many can cause descriptions to get truncated, which means Claude might mismatch your instruction to a Skill.

Here’s a really useful tip: if you have Skills that are manually triggered (like deploy, commit, etc.), add disable-model-invocation: true in the SKILL.md, and Claude won’t see those Skill descriptions at all. They only load when you manually invoke /skill-name, taking zero Context. I covered this fully in Agent Skills: More Token-Efficient Than MCP — Demoed With Claude Code.

Letting conversations go too long without cleaning

This is probably the gotcha most people hit. People do a lot of different things in a single conversation — fix feature A, then feature B, ask a few unrelated questions in between — and the conversation history gets longer and longer, Context gets fuller and fuller. Worse, if you keep correcting Claude’s mistakes, those failed attempts stay in Context too, which actually messes with Claude’s later reasoning.

“Just use /compact to compress, right?” you might think.

But /compact is actually pretty unreliable. When it compresses, it easily drops important details — which files you changed, current task progress, test results, etc. After compression, Claude might forget what it did before.

So the approach I’d actually recommend is manually save + restart conversation. The flow looks like this:

  1. When Context hits about 60% (use /context to check), or when you’ve just finished a feature
  2. Have Claude enter Plan Mode (press Shift+Tab twice)
  3. Ask it to save current progress, modified files, todo list, etc. to a Markdown file
  4. Use /clear to wipe the entire conversation
  5. Start a new conversation, ask Claude to read that Markdown file and continue

For example, you could tell Claude something like:

After saving, run /clear, then start a new conversation with:

This lets you seamlessly pick up where you left off, and you can precisely control what info to keep and what to drop. Once you clear the conversation, those failed attempts and irrelevant chatter get cleared too — Claude’s attention is more focused.

A few other small tips: between different tasks, just /clear and start over — no need to save. For small questions, use /btw — the answer shows up in a floating window without going into conversation history, so it doesn’t take Context. And if you’ve failed to correct Claude two times in a row, stop trying — just /clear, restart, and bake what you learned into the new Prompt. It’ll be faster.

What is AGENTS.md?

Now let’s talk about something related to CLAUDE.md: AGENTS.md.

You might wonder: I already have CLAUDE.md, why do I need AGENTS.md?

Here’s the background. There are more and more AI dev tools out there now, and each tool has its own rule file format. Claude Code uses CLAUDE.md, Cursor uses .cursor/rules/*.mdc, GitHub Copilot uses .github/copilot-instructions.md. If your team has people using Claude Code, others using Cursor, others using Copilot, you need to maintain several different rule files in different formats — and the content is all about the same. That’s a real waste of time.

AGENTS.md was born to solve this. In short, it’s a cross-tool universal standard — a Markdown file in your project root that any tool supporting this standard can read.

AspectCLAUDE.mdAGENTS.md
ScopeOnly Claude Code reads itCross-tool universal standard
Supported toolsClaude CodeCodex CLI, Cursor, Copilot CLI, Gemini CLI, etc.
Special features@imports, hierarchical loading, path rules, /init generationBasic Markdown instructions
Claude Code supportNative readingWon’t auto-read, must import via CLAUDE.md

One important thing here: Claude Code doesn’t auto-read AGENTS.md — it only knows CLAUDE.md. So if your project has AGENTS.md, you need to use @ syntax in CLAUDE.md to import it:

So when do you use which? For me personally, if your team is all on Claude Code, you don’t really need AGENTS.md — just use CLAUDE.md. But if your project uses multiple AI tools, AGENTS.md is a better choice. Put shared rules in AGENTS.md and have each tool import it plus add its own specific settings — at least you don’t have to update rules in multiple places every time.

Practical Context management tips

We’ve talked about a lot of problems above. Here are some practical tips you can actually use.

Use Subagents for exploration

If you need Claude to do exploratory work — like “look at the architecture of this codebase” — use a Subagent. What does that mean? A Subagent has its own independent Context Window. It can read a bunch of files, do the analysis, and return only a concise summary to your main conversation. That way your main Context doesn’t get blown out with file contents.

Use path-scoped rules

Put rules that only apply to specific directories under .claude/rules/, using paths to scope when they load:

This rule only loads when Claude accesses files under src/api/ — otherwise it doesn’t take Context.

Use Plan Mode to save Tokens

Press Shift+Tab twice to switch to Plan Mode. In this mode, Claude thinks through the plan first before doing anything, which usually saves a fair amount of Tokens — it’s not trying things and failing as it goes.

Add a save-progress rule to CLAUDE.md

I mentioned the manual save + restart approach above. You can add a section to CLAUDE.md so Claude knows exactly what to save when you ask:

This way, every time you need to clean Context, just tell Claude “save” and it knows what to do.

Use permissions.deny to exclude unneeded files

If you have files you don’t want Claude Code reading — like .env, secrets/, build/, sensitive or unnecessary stuff — you can exclude them with permissions.deny in .claude/settings.json:

Excluded files disappear from search results, and Claude can’t read them — so unrelated file contents don’t take up Context.

Use Status Line for real-time monitoring

If you want to know how much Context you’re using at any given moment, set up Status Line at the bottom of the terminal to show real-time usage. I covered this in detail in Claude Code Status Line setup guide.

Wrap up

To summarize the key points of this post:

  • AGENTS.md is a cross-tool universal standard — useful when teams mix multiple AI tools. But Claude Code doesn’t auto-read it, so you need to import it via @AGENTS.md in CLAUDE.md.
  • Keep CLAUDE.md under 200 lines, only include stuff Claude can’t infer from the code.
  • Regularly use /context to check Token usage.
  • At ~60% Context or after finishing a feature, manually save + /clear and restart. Don’t rely on Auto Compaction.
  • Disconnect MCP Servers you’re not using. Use /mcp to manage connections.
  • For manually triggered Skills, add disable-model-invocation: true so they don’t take Context.
  • Use Subagents for exploratory work — don’t blow out the main Context.
  • Use path-scoped rules, Plan Mode, and permissions.deny to precisely control Context usage.

Hopefully this gives you a better grasp of Context management. After all, Tokens aren’t cheap — save where you can.
entry.summary: This post is about Claude Code's Context management problem. A lot of people install a bunch of Skills, Plugins, and MCP Servers, then write a giant CLAUDE.md — and before they've even started working, half their Context is already gone. We'll also cover AGENTS.md (a cross-tool universal standard) and how to make sure your Context goes where it actually matters.

entry.title: Is SEO Not Enough? Meet AEO — Getting Your Site Found by AI Search
entry.content:
Intro

This post is a quick walk through AEO (Answer Engine Optimization). With more and more people swapping Google for ChatGPT and Perplexity, the old SEO playbook needs an update — so let’s talk about what AEO actually is, how it differs from SEO, and what you can do about it.

A quick recap on SEO

Before we get into AEO, let’s quickly revisit what SEO is.

SEO stands for Search Engine Optimization. The short version: it’s about getting your site to rank higher on Google, Bing, and other search engines so users can find you more easily.

The usual SEO playbook covers:

  • Keyword optimization: make sure your content includes the terms users actually search for
  • meta tags: dial in the title, description, etc., so search engines understand what your page is about
  • Backlinks: get other sites to link to you to boost authority
  • Site speed and experience: load times, mobile-friendliness, all of which impact rankings
  • Sitemap and robots.txt: tell search engines about your site structure and which pages should be indexed

Personally, I’ve written a few SEO-related posts before — like The three Core Web Vitals that matter for SEO: LCP, FID, CLS (article in Mandarin) and A quick guide to robots.txt (article in Mandarin) — go check them out if you’re curious.

So at its core SEO is about ranking your site as high as possible on the results page. Higher rank = more clicks = more traffic. That logic has held for over a decade — but things are starting to shift, and that shift is where AEO comes in.

So what’s AEO?

Picture this scenario for a second:

In the old days, if you wanted to know “what is CSR?” you’d open Google, type the keyword, and pick a decent-looking article from the results. But these days? A lot of people just open ChatGPT or Perplexity, ask “what is CSR?”, and the AI hands them a clean answer — they never click through to anyone’s site.

Which raises a problem: if users aren’t clicking into your site anymore, what good is your SEO?

AEO stands for Answer Engine Optimization. “Answer engine” here means the AI tools that answer your questions directly — ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, Bing Copilot, and so on.

So AEO is about…

『Making your content the source AI cites when it answers questions.』

Here’s an analogy that might help. Traditional SEO is like getting your book onto the most visible shelf in the bookstore — people who walk in might notice it. AEO is like getting your book quoted by the teacher in class — every student sees it whether they ever set foot in the bookstore or not.

Note
While researching this stuff you might also bump into terms like GEO (Generative Engine Optimization), LLMO, AIO, and friends. These are all basically describing the same thing, and the industry hasn’t really settled on strict definitions, so I’m sticking with AEO throughout this post.

Why should you care about AEO?

You might be thinking: my SEO is doing fine, why bother with AEO?

Let’s start with a phenomenon called the zero-click search. What does that mean? It means the user gets their answer right on the search results page — they never click into anyone’s site.

How dramatic is this? Around 70% of Google searches are now zero-click, and the number is still climbing.

And that’s just regular Google. Throw AI search into the mix and it gets wilder — ChatGPT has hundreds of millions of weekly active users at this point, and Perplexity’s traffic has exploded several-fold over the past year.

So the way users get information is shifting. The old story was: write a post, do SEO, Google ranks you near the top, users click through. The new story is: user asks AI a question, AI hands them the answer, your post never even gets a chance to be seen. QQ

The good news is, when your content does get cited by AI, the upside is real — pages cited by AI Overviews tend to see meaningful boosts in organic clicks, and visitors coming via AI recommendations convert noticeably better than typical organic traffic.

So AEO isn’t just a trend — it directly affects your traffic and conversion.

How SEO and AEO differ

Here’s a table to make the contrast easier to see:

DimensionTraditional SEOAEO
GoalRank as high as possible on results pageGet cited as a source by AI
User behaviorBrowse results, click linksRead AI’s answer directly
Optimization unitPage level (title, meta, backlinks)Fact level (extractable claims and data)
Content styleLong-form, keyword-denseStructured, answer-first per paragraph
Technical focusmeta tags, sitemap, page speedSchema Markup, structured data
MetricsRank, traffic, click-through rateCitation count, brand mention rate
Success indicatorRank #1 on GoogleBecome a trusted source AI cites

One important observation: less than 40% of sources cited by AI Overviews come from Google’s top 10 ranked pages. Translation — being #1 on Google does not guarantee AI will cite you, because AI cares about slightly different signals than traditional search engines.

How do AI search engines decide who to cite?

Before we talk about how to do AEO, we need to understand how AI decides whose content to cite.

Most AI search engines run on an architecture called RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation). If RAG is new to you, my earlier post Heard of LLM, RAG, and Prompt? A walkthrough of common Generative AI terminology (article in Mandarin) has a fuller explanation.

The short version of RAG looks like this:

  1. The user asks a question
  2. The AI system searches for documents related to that question
  3. The relevant documents are filtered by authority, content quality, and other criteria — usually only the top few survive
  4. The language model generates an answer based on those filtered docs and cites the sources

Different platforms do this slightly differently. ChatGPT defaults to answering from the model’s own knowledge, and only fetches the web when search is enabled. Perplexity searches the web on every answer and shows citations by default. Google AI Overviews plugs into Google’s own search index and knowledge graph.

So what does AI look at when filtering sources? Typically these dimensions:

  • Trustworthiness: is your site a credible source
  • Content quality: is the structure clear, are claims backed up, does it actually answer the question
  • Freshness: is the content updated regularly
  • Structure: clear headings, lists, tables — anything that makes content easier for AI to extract

Now that you know how it works under the hood, let’s get into what you can actually do.

How to do AEO

Lead with the answer, add context after

This is the single most important AEO writing principle.

When AI extracts content from your page, it usually only looks at the first sentence or two of each paragraph. If you open with a long ramp-up or background story, AI will likely skip past you and find a source that answers the question more directly.

So every paragraph should start by directly answering the core question of that paragraph, with explanation and context coming after.

For example, suppose you’re introducing Schema Markup:

The not-so-good version:

Before we discuss Schema Markup, let’s talk about the history of structured data. Back in 2011, Google, Bing, and Yahoo jointly launched schema.org… (and only then gets to what Schema Markup actually is)

The better version:

Schema Markup is structured data markup added to HTML to help search engines and AI understand your content. The common format is JSON-LD, which you can use to tag article authors, publish dates, FAQ entries, and more.

See it? The second version tells you upfront what Schema Markup is — AI can grab that paragraph as-is to answer a user’s question.

Make every paragraph stand on its own

AI parses content paragraph by paragraph, not at the article level. So each paragraph should be able to answer one question on its own — even if it’s lifted out of context.

The recommended approach is to give every H2/H3 heading a clear semantic — ideally one question or one concept — keep paragraphs to a reasonable length, and lean on lists and tables to organize information. AI has an easier time grabbing the key points that way.

Add structured data (Schema Markup)

Schema Markup is one of the most important ways to make AI understand your content. Pages with proper Schema Markup are noticeably more likely to get cited.

For blog posts, I’d recommend the following Schemas:

  • Article / BlogPosting: marks your article’s title, author, publish date, etc.
  • FAQPage: if your post has Q&A-style sections, this Schema lets AI extract Q&A pairs directly. Worth noting — Google did limit FAQ rich results in 2023, but FAQPage Schema is still a strong signal for AI search engines
  • BreadcrumbList: marks your site’s hierarchy
  • Person + Organization: marks the author and their organization

Schema Markup is written in JSON-LD and goes inside <head>. If you’re on Hexo or WordPress, there’s usually a plugin that generates it automatically — you don’t have to write it by hand.

Back your content with data where appropriate

AI prefers content backed by data over subjective takes. So sprinkle in concrete data and citations where it makes sense, and link to the original sources.

Compare “AI search is growing fast and more people are using it” with something like “according to stats, Perplexity’s traffic more than doubled in the past year.” The second one is way more likely to get pulled into an AI answer as supporting evidence.

Build topical authority

AI prefers sources that have deep coverage on a topic. What does that mean? Imagine you’ve written one article about SEO, while another site has a full series covering SEO, AEO, Core Web Vitals, Schema Markup, robots.txt, and more. AI will lean toward the second site, because it’s demonstrated broader and deeper knowledge of the subject.

So I’d recommend building topic clusters around your core topics — one pillar post linked to multiple supporting posts. Personally, I’ve written The three Core Web Vitals that matter for SEO: LCP, FID, CLS (article in Mandarin), A quick guide to robots.txt (article in Mandarin), and there’s also a section on SEO inside the Hexo series (article in Mandarin). All of these cross-link to each other so AI can recognize the depth on this topic.

Update your content regularly

AI search engines significantly favor fresh content for time-sensitive queries — content cited by AI tends to be much newer than what shows up in traditional search. So at minimum, revisit your important posts every quarter, update the date, and refresh any data points.

llms.txt: a sitemap for AI

Most of what we covered above is about how you write your content. There’s also a more technical play — drop an llms.txt file on your site.

What is llms.txt?

If you’ve read my earlier A quick guide to robots.txt (article in Mandarin), you know robots.txt tells search engine crawlers which pages they can and can’t crawl. llms.txt does the opposite.

robots.txt is telling crawlers “you can’t come in.” llms.txt is telling AI “hey, the good stuff is over here, come look.”

llms.txt is a draft proposal introduced by Jeremy Howard, co-founder of Answer.AI, in 2024. The core idea is…

『Proactively tell AI what important content lives on your site.』

Why do we need this? Because when AI reads your webpage, it runs into noise — navigation bars, ads, JavaScript, all kinds of clutter — and pulling out the actually-valuable content is hard. AI’s context window is also finite, so it can’t just slurp your entire site. And AI may land on a single article without any way to know what other valuable content you have.

So llms.txt provides a Markdown-formatted site guide that proactively tells AI your site structure and where the key content is.

Quick comparison between robots.txt and llms.txt:

Dimensionrobots.txtllms.txt
PurposeTell crawlers which pages not to crawlTell AI which content is most important
MindsetExclusion-driven (Disallow)Discovery-driven (good stuff here)
FormatPlain text directivesMarkdown
AudienceTraditional search crawlersLLMs

What does the llms.txt format look like?

The format is simple — it’s just a Markdown file:

A few key points:

  • H1 (required): your site or project name
  • Blockquote description (recommended): describe what your site is about in a sentence or two
  • H2 sections (optional): group your content by topic, with links and short descriptions under each
  • Optional section (optional): links here are non-essential — AI reads them only if it needs more

It’s also recommended to point links to .md files, since Markdown is easier for AI to parse than HTML.

llms.txt vs llms-full.txt

Beyond llms.txt you might also see llms-full.txt. Short version: llms.txt is like a book’s table of contents, while llms-full.txt is the entire book. The first lets AI quickly understand what’s on your site; the second packs the full content of all docs in so AI can read everything end-to-end.

I’d recommend doing both — llms.txt as the index for fast lookup, and llms-full.txt for when AI needs to dig in.

My blog as an example

Here’s roughly what llms.txt looks like for my blog:

Each link gets a short description telling AI what the page is about, so AI can find content relevant to the user’s question faster.

How do you implement this on Hexo?

If you’re on Hexo like me, there’s no dedicated plugin to auto-generate llms.txt yet, but the implementation is pretty simple.

The most direct approach: drop an llms.txt file under source/. When Hexo builds, it copies that file straight to public/, so it’ll be accessible at https://yourdomain/llms.txt.

Heads up though — Hexo by default renders Markdown files into HTML, so you need to add this file to skip_render in _config.yml:

That tells Hexo to leave the file alone and just copy it to the output directory as-is.

If you don’t want to maintain it manually, you could also write a small Node.js script that runs after hexo generate and scans your posts to produce llms.txt — conceptually similar to what hexo-generator-sitemap does.

Does AI actually read llms.txt?

To be honest — llms.txt is still very early-stage. It’s not an official standard.

If you want to know whether AI is actually reading your llms.txt, you can check server logs or analytics tools. Some site owners have spotted OpenAI’s crawlers (OAI-SearchBot, ChatGPT-User) hitting llms.txt, but a hit doesn’t mean the AI is actually using it when answering questions.

Google has been more reserved on this. Google’s John Mueller even compared llms.txt to the long-deprecated meta keywords tag, suggesting no AI system really uses it right now.

That said, it’s interesting that several hundred sites already implement llms.txt, including Anthropic, Stripe, Cloudflare, Vercel, Supabase, and other well-known names. So even if the effect is unproven, the industry is at least taking it seriously.

My take: setting up llms.txt takes very little time — basically zero cost. Even if AI doesn’t officially support it today, there’s a high chance support comes later, so you might as well put one up and let your site be ready in advance.

Common AEO terminology

If this is your first time touching AEO, the jargon can feel a bit much. Here are the common ones.

E-E-A-T

Stands for Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness — the framework Google uses to evaluate content quality. AI search engines look at similar signals when picking sources to cite.

The short version: AI checks whether you (or your site) have enough experience and expertise in this domain, whether others recognize your content, and whether your content is trustworthy. So if your site has clear author info, relevant credentials, and backlinks from other authoritative sites, your E-E-A-T score is higher.

Schema Markup

Already covered earlier — basically structured data added to HTML, usually in JSON-LD format, that machine-readably tells search engines and AI what your content is, who wrote it, when it was published, and so on.

Featured Snippets

That big box at the top of a Google results page that displays an answer directly. Pages with a Featured Snippet typically pull in more traffic than other ranked pages, and AEO strategy also boosts your odds of landing one — so doing AEO helps traditional SEO too.

AI Overviews

The AI-generated summary Google now shows at the top of search results. The frequency at which it shows up has been steadily increasing. AI Overviews stitches information from multiple sources, and when it appears, click-through rates on traditional search results drop significantly.

Zero-Click Search

The user gets the answer they wanted from the results page or AI’s response, without clicking into any site. Around 70% of Google searches are zero-click today.

Topical Authority

The depth and breadth a site demonstrates on a particular topic, built up through topic clusters and consistent output of related content. AI prefers sites with deep coverage on specific topics.

Are there tools to track AEO performance?

If you’re doing AEO, you probably want to track results. There are a few tools that help:

  • Semrush AI Toolkit: track your visibility in AI search and compare against competitors
  • Ahrefs Brand Radar: track brand mentions on AI platforms
  • HubSpot AEO Grader: free tool that grades how AEO-ready your content is
  • Google Search Console: not AEO-specific, but you can see impressions coming from AI Overviews

The most direct way is the manual one — go to ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini and search for your target keywords yourself, and see whether the AI’s answer cites your content. It’s the most low-tech method, but also the most direct.

AEO and SEO are complementary

One last thing: AEO isn’t here to replace SEO — it’s an extension of it.

The core strategies overlap heavily. Clear content structure, answering user questions, structured data, good page experience — all of these lift both sides. And AI search engines lean on the same signals traditional search cares about — domain authority, content quality, backlinks, etc. — so the stronger your SEO foundation, the better your AEO results tend to be.

The current advice is to keep doing SEO while folding AEO thinking into your content strategy. Per Gartner’s projections, traditional search volume will drop significantly over the next few years, while generative AI search usage will keep climbing.

The earlier you start positioning, the better your odds of holding ground in the AI-search world.
entry.summary: This post is a quick walk through AEO (Answer Engine Optimization). With more and more people swapping Google for ChatGPT and Perplexity, the old SEO playbook needs an update — so let's talk about what AEO actually is, how it differs from SEO, and what you can do about it.

entry.title: Which Claude Code Files Should You Commit? CLAUDE.md, Settings, Rules, Skills — A Complete Breakdown
entry.content:
Intro

If you’ve already been using Claude Code for development, you’ve probably hit this problem: your project suddenly has a bunch of files you’ve never seen before — CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json, .claude/rules/, etc. — and now you’re hesitating about whether to commit any of this.

For me personally, the first time I used Claude Code on a team project, I really hesitated about whether to push CLAUDE.md. After all, my teammates weren’t necessarily using Claude Code — would pushing it be weird? Then I found that the project also had settings.json and settings.local.json — the two looked really similar, so what’s the difference?

Once I dug into Claude Code’s settings architecture, I realized it’s actually been designed to clearly separate “team-shared” from “personal preferences”. As long as you understand the layering, you don’t need to hesitate at all about what to commit.

So this post is about Claude Code’s settings file layers and load order. Once you understand that, you’ll know where each file fits.

Claude Code’s settings layers

Before talking about individual files, let’s understand Claude Code’s overall settings architecture — that’s the key to deciding what to commit.

Claude Code’s settings aren’t a single layer — they’re divided into four:

LayerWho configures itScope
Managed (org level)IT / DevOps adminsEveryone in the org
User (user level)YouAll your projects
Project (project level)The teamEveryone on this project
Local (machine level)YouThis project, just you

In short, Managed is rules forced by company admins, User is your personal cross-project preferences, Project is shared team standards for the project, Local is your personal settings for a specific project.

This layering runs through CLAUDE.md, Settings, and Rules — all three settings systems. So just remember one principle: anything inside the project directory and meant for the team gets committed; anything in your Home directory or with local in the name doesn’t get committed.

Let me walk through each file with this framework.

CLAUDE.md load order

CLAUDE.md is Claude Code’s most central rules file — but it’s not just for the project root. You can put it in several places, and Claude reads them all and merges them at startup.

A real example makes this easier to understand. Say you run Claude Code from my-project/packages/api/. It loads the following CLAUDE.md files in order:

  1. Managed level (if your company has set it up)
  2. ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (your user level)
  3. my-project/CLAUDE.md (project root)
  4. my-project/packages/CLAUDE.md (intermediate directory, if any)
  5. my-project/packages/api/CLAUDE.md (the directory you ran the command from)

A diagram makes it clearer:

Left to right is load order. The further right, the higher the priority — and the brighter the green. So if your user-level CLAUDE.md says “respond in English” but the project’s CLAUDE.md says “respond in Traditional Chinese”, Claude follows the project.

Also, CLAUDE.md files in subdirectories (like src/components/CLAUDE.md) don’t all get loaded at startup — Claude dynamically loads them when it accesses files in that directory, so the entire project’s CLAUDE.md doesn’t get crammed into Context all at once.

So which ones should you commit? Easy — any CLAUDE.md inside the project gets committed, whether it’s the root one or in a subdirectory, because that’s the team-wide standard. The user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is in your Home directory anyway — not in the Repo — so no need to worry about it.

If you don’t know how to write CLAUDE.md, check out my earlier post CLAUDE.md and Rules for AI writing tips (article in Mandarin).

So where do I put my personal preferences?

This is a question lots of people have: the project already has a CLAUDE.md — where do my own preferences go? Should I just modify the project’s CLAUDE.md?

Don’t do that. Personal preferences should go in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. That’s your user-level config and applies to all your projects, and it’s not in any Repo, so it won’t affect your teammates.

For example, the project’s CLAUDE.md might look like this:

Your personal ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md can contain your own preferences:

Claude reads both — team standards and personal preferences both apply. If they conflict, the project level has higher priority, so your personal preferences won’t override team standards.

How Settings get merged

The Settings concept is similar to CLAUDE.md — also layered — but with an extra Local layer.

Looking at the actual files:

  • ~/.claude/settings.json → your personal global settings (User layer)
  • .claude/settings.json → team-shared project settings (Project layer)
  • .claude/settings.local.json → your personal settings for this project (Local layer)

Priority from highest to lowest: Managed > Local > Project > User.

Here’s an important detail: Settings array values aren’t overridden — they’re merged.

A concrete example makes this clearer. Say the project’s .claude/settings.json allows two commands:

And your personal ~/.claude/settings.json allows one more:

Claude Code doesn’t override your personal settings with the project’s — it merges both, so you end up with all three allowed commands: npm run lint, npm run test, and npm run dev.

This design lets the team set shared baseline permissions, with each person adding their own on top — without interfering with each other.

So which one should you commit? .claude/settings.json is team-shared — commit it. .claude/settings.local.json is personal — don’t commit it. And if you used claude init to initialize, it’s already added to .gitignore for you.

The Rules directory

Rules is Claude Code’s modular rules system. You can split rules into multiple .md files under .claude/rules/ — easier to manage than stuffing everything into one CLAUDE.md.

Like CLAUDE.md, Rules are layered:

  • ~/.claude/rules/*.md → your personal rules common across all projects
  • .claude/rules/*.md → team-shared project rules

User-level Rules load first, project-level Rules load after with higher priority.

A neat feature of Rules is you can use paths to scope where they apply:

Rules without paths get fully loaded at startup. Rules with paths only load when Claude is processing files matching those paths — saves Tokens and keeps unrelated rules from interfering.

The project’s .claude/rules/*.md should be committed. Personal ~/.claude/rules/ is in your Home directory and you don’t need to worry about it.

The Skills directory

Skills is Claude Code’s skill-pack system. You can package the knowledge and procedures for a specific task into a standalone Skill, and Claude automatically loads it when relevant.

Like Rules, Skills are also two-layered:

  • ~/.claude/skills/ → your personal Skills common across all projects
  • .claude/skills/ → team-shared project Skills

Each Skill is a folder that contains at least a SKILL.md:

For teams, things like the Code Review process, pre-deploy checklists, or framework-specific dev standards — you make these into Skills under the project’s .claude/skills/. The whole team can use them, so they should be committed.

For personal Skills (like your own habitual debug flow), put them under ~/.claude/skills/ — not in the Repo.

If you want to dig into Skills details and how to write them, check out Agent Skills: More Token-Efficient Than MCP — Demoed With Claude Code.

Auto Memory

Auto Memory files live under ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/ — that’s your Home directory, not in the Repo. They don’t show up in git status at all, so there’s nothing to do.

If you want to learn more about Auto Memory, check out Claude Code Auto Memory complete guide.

What if my teammate doesn’t use Claude Code?

This is something a lot of people worry about: I pushed CLAUDE.md and .claude/ up, but my teammate isn’t using Claude Code at all — does it affect them?

Not at all. To someone not using Claude Code, CLAUDE.md is just a plain Markdown file. .claude/settings.json is just a JSON file sitting there quietly — no side effects on any dev tool.

Conversely, committing this stuff has an upside: if your teammate ever decides to try Claude Code, they can clone and instantly use the existing rules — no setup from scratch.

Recommended .gitignore settings

Actually you only need to add one line:

Everything else personal (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, ~/.claude/settings.json, ~/.claude/rules/, Auto Memory) is in your Home directory, not in the Repo — no need to add it to .gitignore.

If you initialized with claude init, this line is usually already added.

A reminder about sensitive info

Last reminder — whether it’s CLAUDE.md or Rules, do not put API Keys, Tokens, passwords, or anything like that inside.

These config files don’t usually have this problem because they hold rules and preferences, not values. But if you write something like “use this API Key to call XXX” inside CLAUDE.md, that’s risky.

If you really need to reference sensitive info in your rules, use environment variables. Write “use environment variable XXX_API_KEY“ — don’t put the actual value in.

Wrap up

Back to the original question — Claude Code’s settings architecture has actually already drawn a clear line between team and personal. Anything under the project’s .claude/ (settings.json, rules/, skills/) plus the project’s CLAUDE.md is team-shared — commit it. Anything with local in the name (settings.local.json) is personal — don’t commit it. Everything under ~/.claude/ is in your Home directory — has nothing to do with Git.

For personal preferences, don’t try to cram them into the project’s settings. Claude Code’s user level (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, ~/.claude/settings.json, ~/.claude/rules/) is designed for personal preferences — write them there. They apply to all your projects and don’t affect your teammates.
entry.summary: After you start using Claude Code, your project ends up with a bunch of new files — CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json, rules, skills, and so on. Which ones should you commit? Which are personal settings that shouldn't be pushed up? This post walks through Claude Code's multi-tier settings architecture and load order, so you understand the line between personal and team.

entry.title: What Is Claude Code Auto Memory? A Complete Guide to the Auto Memory Feature
entry.content:
Intro

If you’ve already been using Claude Code for development, you’ve probably hit this situation: every time you open a new conversation, Claude has total amnesia and you have to re-explain all the preferences you already told it about.

For me personally, I used to constantly tell Claude not to throw too many emojis into articles, but the next conversation it would start adding them again. Pretty annoying.

I previously wrote a post on CLAUDE.md writing tips (article in Mandarin) covering how to manually configure project rules with CLAUDE.md, but Claude Code’s memory system isn’t just CLAUDE.md. There’s also a feature called Auto Memory that automatically jots down your corrections and preferences during conversation, so you don’t have to repeat yourself next time.

What’s the difference between Auto Memory and CLAUDE.md?

Quick rundown of the difference between these two before we dive in, because a lot of people get confused about what each one is for.

CLAUDE.mdAuto Memory
Who writes itYouClaude writes it automatically
Content typeInstructions and rulesLearned experiences and patterns
ScopeProject, user, or organizationPer Working Tree
LoadingFully loaded every conversationOnly the first 200 lines or 25KB
Best used forCode style, workflow, project structureBuild commands, debug notes, preferences

In short, CLAUDE.md is rules you actively write for Claude — things like “this project uses 2-space indent”, “commit messages in English”, and so on. Auto Memory is what Claude picks up from interacting with you. You don’t have to write it — it remembers automatically.

If you want to learn more about CLAUDE.md, check out my earlier post CLAUDE.md and Rules for AI writing tips (article in Mandarin) — I won’t repeat that here.

What is Auto Memory?

Auto Memory is basically letting Claude take its own notes. When you correct Claude’s behavior in a conversation, or give feedback about whether something worked well, Claude evaluates whether that information will be useful in future conversations. If it thinks it’s worth keeping, it saves it automatically.

For example, say you tell Claude in a conversation:

Don’t use too many emojis in articles, it looks too AI

Claude saves that feedback into a memory file. From then on, every new conversation loads that memory, so it remembers you don’t like emojis.

But it doesn’t write to memory in every conversation — Claude judges whether the info is worth keeping. If it’s just a one-off discussion or something irrelevant to future conversations, it won’t bother recording it.

Note
Auto Memory requires Claude Code v2.1.59 or higher. Check with claude --version.

Where is it stored?

There’s something easy to mix up here. Your project will have a .claude/ folder, but Auto Memory files don’t live there. The project’s .claude/ is just for config files (settings.json, settings.local.json) and Rules.

Auto Memory files are stored in your Home directory under ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/.

A concrete example makes this clearer. If your project is at /Users/ray/my-project:

Both paths have .claude in them, but one is in your project folder and the other is in your Home directory — don’t mix them up. So if you go digging in your project’s .claude/ folder and find only settings.local.json, that’s normal. Memory files don’t live there.

The memory path is derived from the Git repo, so subdirectories and Worktrees of the same repo share the same memory. If you’re not in a Git repo, it falls back to the project root path.

Also, everything under ~/.claude/projects/ is machine-local — it doesn’t sync to other machines or cloud environments.

Customizing the storage location

If you want to store memory somewhere else, set autoMemoryDirectory in your config:

Note
This setting only accepts Policy, Local, and User levels — not Project-level .claude/settings.json. The point is to prevent malicious settings in shared projects from redirecting memory somewhere it shouldn’t go.

The memory directory structure

The memory directory mainly has two kinds of things:

MEMORY.md is the index for the whole memory directory. Claude uses it to track what memories exist and which file each one lives in. Taking my own blog project as an example, my current MEMORY.md looks like this:

Then feedback_writing_style.md contains the actual memory content, recording the fact that I don’t like overusing quotes and emojis in articles.

How loading works

Here’s an important detail: only the first 200 lines or first 25KB (whichever is smaller) of MEMORY.md gets loaded at the start of each conversation. Anything past that doesn’t get read. So Claude tries to keep MEMORY.md lean, splitting more detailed content into separate Topic files.

The Topic files (like debugging.md, patterns.md) aren’t all loaded at startup — Claude reads them when it needs them. So even as your memory grows, it doesn’t dump the entire memory directory into Context every time.

This 200-line limit only applies to MEMORY.md. CLAUDE.md, no matter how long, is fully loaded (though shorter usually means better adherence).

How can I tell when Claude is writing memory?
