When you see “Writing memory” or “Recalled memory” in the Claude Code UI, that means Claude is updating or reading the memory files. You don’t need to do anything — it handles it on its own.

How do I manage Auto Memory?

Use the /memory command

/memory is the main entry point for managing memory. You can use it to see what memory and instruction files are loaded in the current conversation, toggle Auto Memory on/off, quickly open the memory folder, or pick any file to open in your editor.

Actively tell Claude to remember or forget

If you want Claude to remember something, just tell it directly in the conversation — for example, “remember: always use pnpm, not npm”. Claude saves that to Auto Memory.

The reverse works too. If a memory is outdated, you can tell Claude “forget the previous rule about pnpm”, and it’ll update or delete the corresponding memory file.

Edit manually

Auto Memory files are just regular Markdown — you can go to the corresponding memory directory under ~/.claude/projects/ anytime and add, modify, or delete them by hand.

Disabling Auto Memory

If you don’t want Claude recording memory automatically, there are three ways to turn it off:

  • Type /memory in a conversation and use the toggle inside
  • Add "autoMemoryEnabled": false to your config file
  • Set the environment variable CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_AUTO_MEMORY=1

Common questions

How do I see what Auto Memory has saved?

Type /memory and pick the memory folder option to browse — they’re all Markdown files, just open them up.

When should I use Auto Memory vs CLAUDE.md?

Quick guideline: stable, definite rules (code style, naming conventions, build process) belong in CLAUDE.md so the whole team shares them. Personal stuff, or stuff Claude picks up from interacting with you (your preferences, the gotchas you’ve hit, the way you debug a particular bug) — let Auto Memory handle that automatically.

Will Auto Memory disappear after /compact?

Nope. Auto Memory files are stored on disk — they have nothing to do with Context compression. /compact only compresses the conversation log. After Compact, both MEMORY.md and CLAUDE.md get re-read from file and re-injected back into Context.
entry.summary: Tired of having to re-explain your preferences every time you open a new Claude Code conversation? Claude Code actually has an Auto Memory feature that automatically jots down your corrections and preferences during conversations, so you don't have to repeat yourself next time. This post walks through how Auto Memory works, where it's stored, how it gets loaded, and how to manage it.

entry.title: Claude Code Status Line Setup: Monitor Context Usage and Rate Limit in Real-Time
entry.content:
Introduction

After using Claude Code for a while you’ve probably hit this — mid-conversation your Context gets compacted, or your Rate Limit silently gets used up, and you only find out when Claude Code tells you “you’ll need to wait a bit”.

Personally I used to run into this all the time. Context would suddenly get compacted, Claude would forget what we’d just discussed, and I’d have to re-explain everything. Pretty wasteful. The root cause is that Claude Code doesn’t show this info on screen by default — you have no idea how much Context is left or how much of your Rate Limit you’ve burned through.

Good news though: Claude Code has a built-in feature called Status Line that puts this info at the bottom of your terminal in real time — current model, Context usage percentage, 5-hour and 7-day Rate Limit usage, and so on. Once it’s set up, no more guessing. Let’s walk through how to configure it.

What is Status Line?

In short, Status Line is a status bar at the bottom of your terminal that shows live info about your current Claude Code session.

The mechanism is straightforward: Claude Code passes the session’s data as JSON via stdin to a script you specify, your script parses it and prints one line of text, and Claude Code displays that line at the bottom of the terminal.

So Claude Code provides the data, and you decide what to display by writing the script.

What information can it display?

The JSON Claude Code passes to the script includes the following fields:

CategoryFieldDescription
Modelmodel.display_nameDisplay name, e.g. Opus 4.6 (1M context)
Modelmodel.idModel ID, e.g. claude-opus-4-6
Contextcontext_window.used_percentageContext used percentage
Contextcontext_window.remaining_percentageContext remaining percentage
Contextcontext_window.context_window_sizeTotal Context size
Rate Limitrate_limits.five_hour.used_percentage5-hour usage percentage
Rate Limitrate_limits.seven_day.used_percentage7-day usage percentage
Rate Limitrate_limits.five_hour.reset_at5-hour reset time
Rate Limitrate_limits.seven_day.reset_at7-day reset time
Costcost.total_cost_usdTotal session cost (USD)
Costcost.total_duration_msTotal session duration
Gitgit.branchCurrent Git branch
OthercwdCurrent working directory
OtherversionClaude Code version

Note
The Rate Limit fields only carry data for Claude.ai Pro/Max subscribers. If you log in with an API key, those fields will be empty.

The fastest way to set it up

If you don’t want to write the script yourself, Claude Code provides a handy command. Just type this in the Claude Code input box:

Claude Code will generate the script and update the config for you — no manual work needed.

After running it, Claude Code generates a default script in p10k-ish style that looks roughly like this:

You can see the default script already handles a few things:

  • Working directory: home gets shortened to ~, displayed in cyan
  • Git branch: auto-detected, displayed in green
  • Model name: displayed in yellow
  • Context remaining: dim grey by default, automatically turns red as a warning when below 20%

The overall style mirrors the Powerlevel10k (p10k) terminal theme — if you’re already on p10k, it’ll feel consistent.

Note
If you don’t know what Powerlevel10k is, check out my earlier post Mac OS 終端機美化看這一篇就夠了！ (article in Mandarin) for a full install and setup guide.

The default script is fine for most people, but if — like me — you’re using Powerlevel10k (p10k), you’ll spot a problem: the working directory and Git branch are already in the p10k prompt. Showing them again in Status Line is just wasted space.

And more importantly, the default script is missing a few things I think are the most useful:

  • Rate Limit usage: This is where you actually trip — by the time you find out the 5-hour bucket is empty, it’s too late
  • Three-stage colour warning: The default only has dim and red, no yellow middle zone. By the time it goes red you’re often already past the point of doing something about it
  • Session cost: For developers using API keys, knowing how much the current session has cost is genuinely useful in real time

So next I’ll share the customised version I use. Take it as-is or tweak it for your own needs.

My customised version

Design intent

Before writing the script I thought about which info is “only Status Line can show me”:

InfoAlready in p10k?Show in Status Line?
Working directoryYesNot needed
Git branchYesNot needed
Model nameNoNeeded
Context remainingNoNeeded
Rate LimitNoNeeded
Session costNoNeeded

So my version focuses on these. I’ll also show how to add the directory and Git branch back in later.

For colours, I went with three stages:

  • Green (>= 50%): Safe, nothing to worry about
  • Yellow (21% - 49%): Heads up, start wrapping up or prepping a fresh session
  • Red (<= 20%): Danger, finish what you’re doing fast

The same logic applies to both Context and Rate Limit, so a glance tells you exactly where you stand.

The script

Create ~/.claude/statusline.sh:

After creating it, give it execute permission:

Note
The script depends on jq for JSON parsing. If your system doesn’t have it yet, install with brew install jq on macOS, or apt install jq / yum install jq on Linux.

Update the config

Next, open Claude Code’s config file ~/.claude/settings.json and add:

Restart Claude Code and you’ll see the live status info at the bottom of the terminal.

Colours change based on remaining percentage — green for safe, yellow when you should pay attention, red for almost out. Once it goes red, run /compact to compress Context, or just open a fresh session.

What each segment does

A quick summary of each segment and its colour logic:

SegmentIconColourNotes
Model namenonePurple boldAlways shown, visually distinct from the rest
Context bar\uf2db chipGreen/Yellow/Red10-cell progress bar + percentage, the most intuitive view
5h Rate Limit\uf017 clockGreen/Yellow/RedRemaining quota in the rolling 5-hour window
7d Rate Limit\uf017 clockGreen/Yellow/RedRemaining quota in the rolling 7-day window
Session cost\uf155 dollarDim greyQuiet, but glance-able anytime

Note
Rate Limit and Session cost fields will have different data depending on how you log in. Pro/Max subscribers get Rate Limit data, API key users get Session cost data. The script uses // empty to check, so empty fields just don’t render — no blank segments to worry about.

Not on p10k? Add directory and Git branch back

If your terminal isn’t on p10k or another theme that already shows directory info, you’ll want to change two things:

Step 1: Above the parts="" line under # ── Build output ──, add the directory and Git branch segments:

Note
printf '\uf07b' is used to produce the Nerd Fonts icons (folder and Git branch). Note that macOS’s bundled Bash is 3.2 and doesn’t support $'\uf07b' syntax (that needs Bash 4.4+), but printf '\uf07b' works on every version. If your terminal doesn’t have Nerd Fonts, replace those two lines with ICON_DIR="📁" and ICON_GIT="🔀".

Step 2: Remove parts="" and switch the Model segment to append:

The original looked like this:

Change it to:

Two things to watch for here:

  1. Drop parts="": The directory and Git branch sections already populated parts. If you reset it here, everything before gets wiped out
  2. Model uses $parts$SEP: The original was a plain assignment parts=$(printf ...), which would also overwrite. Switch to append so it joins on to what’s already there

Full version (with directory and Git branch)

If you’d rather not piece it together yourself, here’s the finished version — copy, paste, done:

Community tools

If you’d rather not write a script at all, the community has a few ready-made options:

  • ccstatusline: Supports multiple themes and Powerline styles, highly customisable
  • claude-statusline: Comes with sane defaults, install and go

These are basically wrappers that generate the script for you, just without the writing step.

Why bother setting up Status Line?

You might be thinking: what’s the point of knowing how much Context is left? Claude Code auto-compacts when it fills up anyway.

The thing is — compacting Context means Claude Code drops part of the previous conversation, and that can cause it to forget instructions or topics you discussed earlier. Then you re-explain, and the back-and-forth eats more time than the savings.

If you can see the remaining Context live, you can be proactive — run /compact when it’s getting close to full, or open a fresh session, to avoid losing context.

Same goes for Rate Limit. If you know the 5-hour window is running out, you can prioritise the important task or pace differently, instead of getting blocked unexpectedly.

Wrap-up

Status Line is a small but genuinely useful feature. Once it’s set up you barely notice it’s there, but when you need it, a glance tells you the current state — no more guessing.

If you’re using Claude Code daily like I am, it’s worth the few minutes to get this dialled in.
entry.summary: Tired of finding out your Context got compacted or your Rate Limit got eaten through only after the fact? This post walks through Claude Code's Status Line feature — showing model, remaining context, and rate limit usage live at the bottom of your terminal — plus a customised script with a progress bar and a three-stage colour warning. Copy, paste, done.
